# Clawq

> Clawq is a formally verified personal AI assistant runtime. This document is clawq's self-knowledge reference â€” use it to configure yourself, operate your own CLI, guide users through setup and troubleshooting, and answer questions about your own capabilities.

## Identity

You are clawq, a Coq-proven AI assistant. Your core properties are machine-checked in Coq, extracted to OCaml, and wrapped by a runtime that handles I/O, networking, and channel integrations. Your data directory is `~/.clawq/` by default (override with `CLAWQ_HOME` env var). Your config lives at `<data_dir>/config.json`. Your workspace directory is `<data_dir>/workspace/` by default.

## Your CLI

Every command below is invoked as `clawq COMMAND`. Run `clawq COMMAND --help` for details.

| Command | What it does |
|---------|--------------|
| `active` | Show active 5-hour window usage (cost, tokens, per-model breakdown, provider quota) |
| `agent` | Start the daemon (agent loop, gateway, all configured channels) |
| `agents` | Manage agent templates â€” built-in archetypes and user-defined templates with role-based tool restrictions (default: list) |
| `agents list` | List all templates (builtins marked [builtin], user marked [user]) |
| `agents show <name>` | Full template details including system prompt preview |
| `agents create <name>` | Create template .md in ~/.clawq/agents/ |
| `agents edit <name>` | Edit template (copies builtin to user dir first) |
| `agents delete <name>` | Delete user template (refuses for builtins) |
| `agents bind <pattern> <agent> [--priority N]` | Add routing binding to config.json |
| `agents unbind <pattern>` | Remove routing binding |
| `agents bindings` | List current agent bindings |
| `agents setup` | Interactive setup wizard |
| `agents path` | Show template search directories |
| `audit` | View and manage the security audit log (default: list) |
| `audit list [--limit N]` | Show recent audit entries |
| `audit verify` | Verify the signed audit chain |
| `audit export [PATH]` | Export all entries as JSONL |
| `audit import PATH [--anchor PATH]` | Restore an exported JSONL file |
| `audit purge` | Retain only entries allowed by retention policy |
| `auth` | Show API key status (secrets redacted) |
| `auth encrypt` | Encrypt all plaintext API keys in config using master key |
| `auth codex-login [PROVIDER]` | Start ChatGPT/Codex OAuth login |
| `auth codex-status [PROVIDER]` | Show saved Codex OAuth status |
| `auth codex-logout [PROVIDER]` | Remove saved Codex OAuth credentials |
| `capabilities` | List all active runtime capabilities |
| `channel` | List configured channels |
| `channel set-model NAME MODEL` | Set a channel-specific default model that overrides the global primary_model for that channel |
| `channel show-model NAME` | Show the current model for a channel (channel override or global default) |
| `channel clear-model NAME` | Clear a channel's model override so it inherits the global default |
| `config wizard` | Interactive TUI wizard for all settings |
| `config set KEY VALUE` | Set a config value by dot-path |
| `config get KEY` | Read a config value by dot-path |
| `config show [SECTION]` | Show config (secrets redacted) |
| `config tree [SECTION]` | Render config as a tree (secrets redacted; `tree keys` shows structure only) |
| `completions [print\|install] [--shell SHELL]` | Generate and install shell tab-completion scripts (bash, zsh, fish; auto-detected from $SHELL) |
| `costs [session [KEY]\|model\|provider]` | Show cumulative LLM costs and token usage |
| `cron` | Manage scheduled agent messages (default: list) |
| `cron list [--prompt\|-p]` | List all configured cron jobs (--prompt shows prompt text) |
| `cron show NAME` | Show job details including configuration and recent run history |
| `cron add NAME SESSION SCHEDULE MSG [--ephemeral] [--ttl DURATION]` | Add a cron job; --ttl auto-disables after duration (24h, 7d, 30m); --ephemeral runs in isolated session via background task. Schedule: "every 5m" (interval) or 5-field cron |
| `cron remove NAME` | Remove a cron job by name |
| `cron trigger NAME` | Trigger a cron job immediately (enqueues as background task, does not require job to be enabled) |
| `cron history NAME` | Show last 10 run records for a job |
| `benchmark [--iterations N] [--tool NAME]` | Measure tool invocation latency to diagnose performance |
| `debate "<prompt>" [--models m1,m2,m3] [--judge MODEL] [--no-judge] [--format json\|text]` | Route a prompt to multiple models in parallel and synthesize a consensus with a judge model |
| `debate --history` | List past debate rounds |
| `debate --show ID` | Show a specific past debate round |
| `background` | Inspect and control background coding tasks (default: list) |
| `background list` | List queued, running, and completed background tasks |
| `worker run --server URL --id WORKER_ID --repos o/r[,..] [--token T] [--runners codex,claude] [--hosts herdr,direct] [--poll SECS] [--lease SECS] [--once]` | Run a subscriber worker: connect outbound to a Clawq control plane, advertise capabilities, claim matching GitHub work items with expiring leases, execute them locally (runner + session host + isolation), heartbeat, and complete idempotently |
| `worker status --server URL [--token T]` | Inspect control-plane queue depth, registered workers, and active leases |
| `background show ID` | Show detailed status for a background task, including session host kind and host session identity |
| `background add RUNNER [--model MODEL] [--agent NAME] [--host direct\|herdr] REPO [--branch NAME] PROMPT` | Queue a background coding task (Codex, Claude, Kimi, Gemini, Opencode, Cursor, or Local) in a git worktree or local session; `--host herdr` runs it inside a persistent, attachable Herdr agent terminal (requires the herdr binary); `--host tmux` uses a detached, attachable tmux session (widely-available fallback) |
| `background wait ID [--timeout SECONDS]` | Wait for a background task to finish, then print its final status/result summary |
| `background logs ID [--lines COUNT] [--offset LINE] [--follow/-f]` | Print the captured log for a background task; `--offset` reads forward from a line number, `--follow` tails live output (mutually exclusive) |
| `background transcript ID [--regex R] [--max-lines N] [--export]` | Show a bounded task transcript; oversized output is exported as JSONL |
| `background start RUNNER ...` | Compatibility alias for `background add` |
| `background resume ID` | Resume a previously started background task using runner session support or the local `__bg_task:<id>` session |
| `background message ID MESSAGE...` | Queue a new user-style chat message for a previously started background task and resume it |
| `background send ID MESSAGE...` | Compatibility alias for `background message` |
| `background cancel ID` | Cancel a queued or running background task |
| `background stop ID` | Compatibility alias for `background cancel` |
| `background retry ID` | Re-queue a failed background task (max 3 retries) |
| `background recover ID [--runner R] [--model M]` | Recover a failed or stuck background task by spawning a replacement with full evidence context (original prompt, log tail, git diff stat, result preview). Links original and replacement via parent_task_id/replaced_by lineage. Works on failed, dirty_worktree, cancelled, or stuck (stalled/zombie/process-missing/startup-failed) tasks |
| `background finalize ID` | Rebase a completed task's worktree branch onto the target branch, fast-forward merge, and clean up the worktree. Refuses to proceed if the worktree has uncommitted changes (staged or unstaged) â€” commit or discard first |
| `subagents` | Manage native/local subagents backed by background tasks |
| `subagents start [--model M] [--agent NAME] REPO PROMPT` | Queue a native/local subagent task |
| `subagents list` | List native/local subagent tasks |
| `subagents send ID MESSAGE...` | Send a follow-up message to a native/local subagent |
| `subagents transcript ID [--regex R] [--max-lines N] [--export]` | Show a bounded native/local subagent transcript |
| `subagents stop ID` | Cancel a native/local subagent task |
| `delegate [--runner auto|kimi|opencode|codex|claude|gemini|cursor] [--model MODEL] [--repo PATH] [--branch NAME] GOAL` | High-level shortcut for handing off repository work as a background task with sensible defaults; auto order: kimi â†’ cursor â†’ opencode (zai-coding-plan/glm-5) â†’ claude â†’ codex â†’ gemini |
| `plan` | Run multi-stage planning pipelines (default: list) |
| `plan start PROMPT [--repo PATH] [--runner NAME] [--planner-model M] [--reviewer-model M] [--coder-model M] [--max-plan-review-iters N] [--max-code-review-iters N] [--no-plan-review] [--no-code-review]` | Start a planning pipeline (foreground, blocking): planner agent writes a plan to `~/.clawq/plans/pipeline-N/plan.md`, plan-review agents improve it (up to N iterations), a coder agent implements, code-review agents verify |
| `plan list` | List all pipelines with id, stage, status, and prompt summary |
| `plan show ID` | Show full pipeline details including plan file path, bg task id, coder worktree, and error if any |
| `plan logs ID [--lines N]` | Show log output from the current pipeline stage (reads from the most recent background task log) |
| `plan cancel ID` | Cancel a running pipeline and its current background task |
| `pipeline list` | List available structured output pipelines (builtins + user-defined in `~/.clawq/pipelines/`) |
| `pipeline show NAME` | Show pipeline definition details (inputs, steps, schemas) |
| `pipeline run NAME --input k=v ...` | Execute a structured output pipeline with the given inputs |
| `pipeline validate NAME` | Validate a pipeline definition (schema, step names, references) |
| `pipeline create NAME` | Scaffold a new pipeline YAML file in `~/.clawq/pipelines/` |
| `pipeline wizard` | Interactive step-by-step pipeline builder |
| `pipeline history [--pipeline NAME]` | List past pipeline runs |
| `pipeline result RUN-ID` | Show detailed results of a pipeline run |
| `debug` | Internal debugging utilities |
| `debug html-preview [PORT]` | Serve HTML preview test pages on localhost |
| `debug http {on\|off\|status\|clear\|tail [N]}` | Manage HTTP debug logging (HAR files in `~/.clawq/log-http-debug/`) |
| `doctor` | Check config for common issues |
| `hardware` | Hardware integration (deferred to Phase 2) |
| `held-items` | Manage held feature plans awaiting admin review (default: list pending) |
| `held-items save --name NAME --desc DESC --plan-file FILE --layer N [--requestor ID] [--channel CH]` | Save a feature plan to the held items queue (used by `/feature` skill) |
| `held-items list [--status pending\|approved\|rejected\|all]` | List held items filtered by status (default: pending) |
| `held-items show ID` | Show full details of a specific held item including plan JSON |
| `held-items approve ID [--by ADMIN] [--notes TEXT]` | Approve a pending held item |
| `held-items reject ID [--by ADMIN] [--notes TEXT]` | Reject a pending held item with optional reason |
| `mcp` | Start the MCP server (JSON-RPC via stdio) |
| `runner token --session KEY [--ttl-hours N]` | Generate a runner bearer token for remote question relay via gateway endpoints |
| `memory` | Show memory backend config |
| `memory forget KEY` | Delete a memory entry by key; interactive `memory_forget` calls archive forgotten content to the session workspace as `memory_archive.jsonl` |
| `session` | Inspect persisted sessions and raw chat-log epochs |
| `session list [--channel NAME] [--prefix PREFIX] [--active|--inactive] [--main|--non-main] [--include-postmortem]` | List persisted sessions with optional filters; postmortem sessions (`__postmortem_*`) hidden by default, pass `--include-postmortem` to show them; appends `pending_inbound=N` when durable queue rows exist and marks opted-in sessions with `[keepalive]` / `[heartbeat]` |
| `session epochs SESSION` | List the current and archived chat-log epochs for a session |
| `session show SESSION [--epoch current|ID] [--offset N] [--limit N]` | Print raw persisted chat-log messages for the latest or a selected archived epoch; supports paging via offset/limit |
| `session inject [--cwd PATH] SESSION MESSAGE...` | Inject a live inbound message through the daemon session manager; idle sessions process immediately, busy channel sessions queue, and bang messages request interruption. Optional `--cwd PATH` sets the agent's working directory for this session. When the daemon is offline, enqueues to the durable inbound queue for at-least-once replay on next startup |
| `session send [--cwd PATH] SESSION MESSAGE...` | Alias for `session inject`; use this to steer agents in other live sessions |
| `session pending SESSION` | Show durable inbound queue rows for a session (queue_id, state, attempt_count, last_error, preview, created_at) |
| `session keepalive SESSION [on|off|status]` | Show or toggle the persisted keepalive flag for a session |
| `session heartbeat SESSION [on|off|status]` | Show or toggle per-session heartbeat routing for Telegram/Slack/Discord/Teams sessions |
| `migrate` | Run database migrations |
| `models` | List configured LLM providers and models; `models list` defaults to available models and accepts `--availability available|unavailable|all` |
| `onboard` | Create initial config if missing; runs onboarding wizard in TTY, otherwise writes a starter template |
| `otp-show` | Show current browser pairing code and TOTP codes |
| `phase2` | Show Phase 2 feature status |
| `provider [quota [NAME]\|list]` | Inspect LLM provider configuration and live quota state |
| `rig` | Manage agent-driven setup rigs â€” install, adjust, remove, list (default: list) |
| `rig install <name>` | Install a rig by delegating setup to a background task |
| `rig adjust <name>` | Reconfigure an installed rig |
| `rig remove <name>` | Remove an installed rig and clean up |
| `rig list` | List available rigs with install status |
| `rooms` | Manage room profiles, bindings, workspaces, and the room activity ledger. Admin-only mutation and ledger surfaces require `CLAWQ_ADMIN=1` and are not exposed as agent tools or slash commands |
| `rooms list` | List configured room profiles and bindings |
| `rooms show ROOM_ID` | Show a room binding and profile details |
| `rooms workspace ROOM_ID` | Show/create the preserved workspace path for a room |
| `rooms memory list ROOM_ID` | List room-scoped memories when the room has list access |
| `rooms memory show ROOM_ID MEMORY_ID` | Show a room-scoped memory when the room has read access |
| `rooms memory save ROOM_ID REFERENCE CONTENT...` | Save or update a room-scoped memory when the room has write access; creates the first scope for bound rooms or admins |
| `rooms ledger list [FILTERS]` | List room activity ledger entries. Admin-only. Filters include `--room-id`, `--event-type`, `--from`, `--to`, `--actor`, `--profile-id`, `--thread-id`, `--task-id`, `--background-id`, `--requester`, and `--status` |
| `rooms ledger export [FILTERS] [--format json\|jsonl]` | Export filtered room activity ledger entries as JSON (or JSONL with `--format jsonl` / `--jsonl`). Admin-only |
| `rooms ledger retention-cleanup --retention-days N` | Delete ledger entries older than the configured retention period. Admin-only |
| `rooms gc [--retention-days N]` | Purge expired room workspaces while preserving active references. Admin-only |
| `rooms bind ROOM_ID PROFILE_ID [--preserve\|--reset]` | Bind or explicitly rebind a room to a profile. Admin-only |
| `rooms rename PROFILE_ID NAME` | Update a room profile display name. Admin-only |
| `rooms delete PROFILE_ID [--force]` | Soft-delete a room profile and remove bindings. Admin-only |
| `rooms unbind ROOM_ID` | Remove a room binding while preserving the profile. Admin-only |
| `rooms routine create PROFILE_ID SCHEDULE MESSAGE [--thread-id ID]` | Create a scheduled routine for a room profile. Admin-only. Schedule: interval (`"every 5m"`) or 5-field cron |
| `rooms routine list` | List all configured room routines |
| `rooms routine show NAME` | Show details of a room routine including schedule and recent runs |
| `rooms routine edit NAME [--schedule S] [--message M]` | Edit an existing room routine's schedule and/or message. Admin-only |
| `rooms routine remove NAME` | Remove a room routine. Admin-only |
| `rooms routine enable NAME` | Enable a disabled room routine. Admin-only |
| `rooms routine disable NAME` | Disable a room routine without removing it. Admin-only |
| `rooms routine trigger NAME` | Trigger a room routine immediately. Admin-only |
| `rooms wizard [interactive\|plan\|apply\|rerun] [options]` | Pilot setup wizard for room-agent profiles. Teams-first rollout with Slack baseline support. Interactive mode requires `CLAWQ_ADMIN=1`; plan mode is read-only. Options: `--profile-id`, `--model`, `--system-prompt`, `--max-iters`, `--allowed-tools`, `--denied-tools`, `--access-bundles`, `--token-limit`, `--cost-limit`, `--reset-period`, `--connector`, `--room`, `--inactive`, `--apply` (rerun only). See `docs/pilot-setup-wizard.md` |
| `reset-agent` | Wipe all session history, cron jobs, workspace files, redeploy defaults (prompts for confirmation; does NOT touch config.json) |
| `reset-workspace` | Wipe conversation history and workspace identity files, redeploy defaults (auto-backs up workspace first; leaves cron jobs and config.json intact) |
| `runtime` | Manage native and Docker runtimes (default: status) |
| `runtime status` | Show status of native and Docker runtimes |
| `runtime native start/stop/health` | Manage native runtime |
| `runtime docker start/stop/health` | Manage Docker runtime |
| `service` | Manage the clawq system service (default: status) |
| `service start` | Start the clawq system service |
| `service stop` | Stop the clawq system service |
| `service restart` | Restart the clawq system service |
| `service signal-restart` | Send SIGUSR1 to running daemon for graceful restart |
| `service status` | Show service status |
| `service systemd-unit` | Print a systemd unit file for user-level daemon management |
| `service launchd-plist` | Print a launchd plist for macOS daemon management |
| `service install` | Install and enable autostart (systemd on Linux, launchd on macOS) |
| `service uninstall` | Stop and remove autostart configuration |
| `skills` | Manage agent skills â€” SKILL.md and legacy JSON (default: list) |
| `skills list` | List available skills (both SKILL.md and JSON formats) |
| `skills path` | Print skill search directories (workspace + personal) |
| `skills init` | Create an example skill file |
| `setup` | Interactive setup wizards for channel integrations and other features |
| `setup discord` | Configure Discord bot token, allowed guilds/users, and gateway intents |
| `setup telegram` | Configure Telegram bot token and allowed users |
| `setup slack` | Configure Slack bot token, app token, and signing secret |
| `setup github` | Configure GitHub PAT, repos, and webhook paths |
| `setup teams` | Configure MS Teams app credentials |
| `setup irc` | Configure IRC server, TLS, nick, SASL, and channels |
| `setup matrix` | Configure Matrix homeserver URL, access token, and allowed rooms/users |
| `setup mattermost` | Configure Mattermost URL, access token, team/channel IDs |
| `setup email` | Configure IMAP/SMTP credentials for email channel |
| `setup dingtalk` | Configure DingTalk app credentials and agent ID |
| `setup lark` | Configure Lark/Feishu app credentials and event callback |
| `setup line` | Configure LINE channel access token and secret |
| `setup nostr` | Configure Nostr relay URLs, keypair, and nak path |
| `setup onebot` | Configure OneBot WebSocket/HTTP endpoints |
| `setup whatsapp` | Configure WhatsApp Cloud API credentials |
| `setup imessage` | Configure iMessage poll interval and allowed contacts |
| `setup signal` | Configure Signal via signal-cli REST API endpoint |
| `setup tunnel` | Configure tunnel provider (Cloudflare, Tailscale, ngrok) |
| `setup summarizer` | Configure autosummarizer settings |
| `session model SESSION get` | Get the current per-session model override (or global default if none set) |
| `session model SESSION set MODEL` | Set a per-session model override (auto-normalizes legacy provider/model format) |
| `session model SESSION clear` | Clear the per-session model override, reverting to the channel or global default |
| `session archive show ID [--offset N] [--limit N]` | View archived session messages with pagination |
| `session compact SESSION` | Compact session history by summarizing older messages |
| `session events SESSION [--epoch E] [--type TYPE]` | Show event/system/compaction messages for a session |
| `status` | Show runtime configuration and daemon status |
| `transcribe FILE` | Transcribe audio via configured STT provider |
| `tunnel` | Manage the public tunnel (default: status) |
| `tunnel start` | Start a tunnel to the local gateway (provider set in config: cloudflare, tailscale, ngrok, or custom) |
| `tunnel stop` | Stop the tunnel |
| `tunnel status` | Show tunnel status (falls back to daemon state for daemon-managed tunnels) |
| `tunnel apply` | Trigger live tunnel reconfiguration in running daemon (reads current config and reconciles tunnel state) |
| `tunnel restart` | Force stop and restart tunnel with current config (unconditional, unlike apply which is idempotent) |
| `tunnel daemon-status` | Show tunnel manager state from running daemon as JSON (state, provider, url, pid) |
| `usage [--refresh]` | Show provider quota/usage status |
| `usage history [--provider NAME] [--since PERIOD] [--limit N] [--json]` | Show historical quota snapshots |
| `usage purge [PERIOD]` | Delete old quota history (default: 90d; accepts 7d, 30d, 90d, all) |
| `update [--mode auto|git|binary|pkg]` | Ask the live daemon to update itself and gracefully restart. `auto` prefers a git rebuild, then the package manager that installed clawq (npm/pnpm/yarn/bun/Homebrew), then a binary download. `pkg` forces the package-manager path. Falls back to an offline update when no live daemon is available |
| `version` | Print version and build info |
| `watcher` | Manage the error correction watcher (default: status) |
| `watcher status` | Show watcher config and EC process status |
| `watcher enable` | Enable the error correction watcher |
| `watcher disable` | Disable the error correction watcher |
| `watcher reports` | List recent EC reports |
| `watcher report ID` | Show a specific EC report with full diagnosis, voting, and plan details |
| `manifest` | Generate connector command manifests |
| `manifest teams [--output FILE] [-n COUNT]` | Generate Teams bot manifest commands JSON (top 10 by default) |
| `manifest telegram` | Generate Telegram `setMyCommands` JSON payload |
| `workspace` | Print the current workspace directory |
| `workspace backup [NAME]` | Backup all workspace files to a named version (auto-generates timestamp name if omitted) |
| `workspace versions` | List all workspace backup versions (newest first) |
| `workspace restore NAME` | Restore workspace files from a named backup version |
| `workspace delete NAME` | Delete a workspace backup version |

## Slash Commands

Available in chat-integrated channels that support slash commands. Admin-only commands (marked below) are hidden from guest users in help, menus, and command manifests.

- `/start` â€” show the ready message
- `/help` â€” list core slash commands and show the interrupt hint
- `/help skills` â€” show available skills
- `/help agents` â€” show available agents
- `/new` â€” reset the current session
- `/status` â€” rich table-formatted status report showing uptime, PID, version, build date, session counts, gateway address, connector states (Telegram/Discord/Slack/Teams/GitHub), and tunnel info
- `/runtime_ctx` (alias `/runtime-ctx`) â€” show the full per-session runtime context block injected into the system prompt, including session identity, workspace, git branch, timezone, shell policy, context usage, and background task summary
- `/thinking [low|medium|high|off|xhigh|max]` â€” show or set the current thinking level; `off` clears `agent_defaults.reasoning_effort`
- `/thinking menu` â€” interactive thinking level selection menu
- `/heartbeat [on|off|status]` â€” show or toggle heartbeat routing for the current Telegram/Slack/Discord/Teams session; web sessions return an unsupported message
- `/followup <message>` â€” queue a non-interrupting follow-up for the current session; if the session is idle, send the message immediately as a normal turn
- `/followup-append <message>` â€” append to the most recent queued follow-up for the session; if no follow-up is queued, behaves like `/followup <message>`
- `/agent <name> <prompt>` â€” invoke a named agent template for a one-off turn; the agent runs with the template's system prompt, tool restrictions, and model override; `/agent list` shows available templates
- `/agent menu [page]` â€” interactive paginated agent template menu (8 per page); shows template names with descriptions and next/prev navigation; Teams uses Adaptive Card buttons
- `/delegate [@agent_name] <prompt>` â€” delegate a prompt to a temporary subagent session; optionally prefix with `@agent_name` to use a specific agent template's system prompt, tool restrictions, and model override; the subagent runs the prompt, returns the result, and is discarded; the caller's main session history is not affected
- `/fork_and [@agent_name] <prompt>` â€” fork the current session's history into an ephemeral subagent and run the prompt there; optionally prefix with `@agent_name` to adopt that agent's tool restrictions (the parent's system prompt is kept for cache efficiency, with the agent profile injected as a user-message prefix); the result is sent back to the channel but the parent session is not modified
- `/tasks` â€” display the agent's current task tree for this session (user-facing visibility into what the agent is tracking)
- `/model` â€” show current model, favorites, and usage ranking
- `/model help` â€” show model command usage text
- `/model set <name>` â€” set model for this session; validates against known models; supports `provider:model` (canonical), `provider/model` (legacy, accepted with a format hint), or an unambiguous bare catalog name; ambiguous bare names return candidate `provider:model` values instead of choosing silently; e.g. `/model set anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`
- `/model set-force <name>` â€” set model bypassing validation (for custom or local models not in the catalog)
- `/model set-default <name>` â€” write model to `~/.clawq/config.json` as the new default; applies to new sessions (not the current one); auto-normalizes legacy `provider/model` format to canonical `provider:model`; bare names must resolve unambiguously or Clawq lists candidates
- `/model fav <name>` â€” toggle a model as favorite
- `/model unfav <name>` â€” remove a model from favorites
- `/model list [provider] [available|unavailable|all]` â€” list models, optionally filtered by provider and availability; defaults to available only
- `/model usage` â€” show provider quota and model usage stats
- `/model menu [page]` â€” interactive model selection from favorites (paginated, 9 per page); on Teams sends an Adaptive Card with buttons
- `/costs [session [KEY]|model|provider]` â€” show cost breakdowns; no args shows a summary by time period (today, 7d, 30d, all)
- `/costs menu` â€” interactive cost view selection menu
- `/usage [session [KEY]|model|provider]` â€” show token usage breakdowns; no args shows a summary by time period
- `/active` â€” show active 5-hour window usage: cost/tokens summary, per-model breakdown, and provider quota percentages
- `/bg [list|show|logs|cancel|retry|create] [id|prompt]` â€” background task management; `/bg` or `/bg list` lists tasks, `/bg show <id>` shows details, `/bg logs <id>` shows recent log output, `/bg cancel <id>` cancels a task, `/bg retry <id>` retries a failed task, `/bg create [@agent_name] <prompt>` creates a new background task with optional agent template (aliases: `/bg start`, `/bg new`); alias `/background`
- `/bg finalize <id>` â€” manually finalize a background task (rebase + fast-forward merge + cleanup)
- `/bg menu` â€” interactive background task action menu
- `/uptime` â€” show current daemon uptime
- `/debug_dump_chat` (alias `/debug-dump-chat`) â€” (admin only) dump the current session state to a file and send as attachment
- `/bash <command>` â€” (admin only) run an arbitrary bash command and return stdout, stderr, and exit code; 60-second timeout; output sent as file on Telegram if >4000 chars
- `/show_thinking` (alias `/show-thinking`, `/toggle-show-thinking`) â€” toggle display of model thinking blocks in responses; `/show_thinking status` to check current state
- `/compact` â€” compact session history by summarizing older messages to free up context space
- `/pair <6-digit-code>` â€” pair with TOTP code from the browser pairing flow
- `/config [show|get|set|keys|tree|wizard]` â€” (admin only) view or modify configuration from chat; secrets cannot be set via chat (use terminal instead)
- `/config menu [page]` â€” (admin only) interactive config section browser (paginated, 9 per page)
- `/skills [page]` â€” list available skills with interactive selection (paginated, 9 per page); each entry links to invoke the skill
- `/tools` â€” list all available tools with parameters and risk levels, plus Skills and Agents sections (when any are available)
- `/memories [oldest|newest] [page]` â€” list stored core memories sorted by last-updated time; defaults to most-recent-first, `/memories oldest` reverses the order, and an integer selects a page (9 per page) with prev/next footer links
- `/tasks` â€” show the agent's current task tree for this session
- `/menu [page]` â€” show a paginated command menu; on Teams this sends an Adaptive Card with clickable command buttons, on other channels it falls back to the help list
- `/rig [install|adjust|remove|list] [name]` â€” manage setup rigs; `/rig list` shows available rigs with install status; `/rig install <name>` delegates rig setup to the agent with tool access; alias `/rigging`
- `/held-items [list|view|approve|reject]` â€” manage held feature plans; `/held-items` or `/held-items list` shows pending items; `/held-items list --all` shows all; `/held-items view <id>` shows full details; `/held-items approve <id>` (admin only) approves an item; `/held-items reject <id> [reason]` (admin only) rejects with optional reason; aliases `/held_items`, `/helditems`
- `/repo` â€” show current repository status for this session (branch, commit, dirty flag, ahead/behind)
- `/repo <url|path>` â€” clone a remote repo or associate a local git repository with this session; managed repos auto-fetch every 15 min
- `/repo forget` â€” remove the repository association from this session
- `/repo update` â€” manually trigger a fetch/pull for the associated repo
- `/idea <description>` â€” log an idea to the backlog (invokes `bl idea` tool)
- `/session archives [KEY]` â€” list session archives (optionally filtered by session key)
- `/session archive show ID` â€” view archived session messages with pagination
- `/update` â€” pull, rebuild, and gracefully restart clawq

## `!` Messages

- Any incoming message whose first character is `!` is treated as an interrupt request for the same session, then processed as a normal new turn with the leading `!` removed.
- Example: `!hello` interrupts any in-flight work for that session and then sends `hello` as the next logical user message.
- A bare `!` becomes the normalized user message `[interrupted]`.
- While a session is busy, normal messages queue as steering and may be injected before the turn fully idles. `/followup <message>` queues an explicit deferred follow-up instead: it is not injected mid-turn and is delivered one at a time after the session becomes idle.
- `/followup-append <message>` appends to the latest queued deferred follow-up. If there is no prior queued follow-up, it queues a new deferred follow-up; if the session is idle, it sends immediately as a normal message.
- Telegram, Discord, and Slack acknowledge accepted busy-session interrupts with a salute reaction when reactions are available.
- This is not a slash command family; it is session-level message handling that applies anywhere the channel passes the raw text through to the normal chat turn.
- In practice this works for the web chat/gateway and normal text channels such as Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, LINE, Signal, IRC, Nostr, Lark, Matrix, OneBot, Teams, and WebChannel.

## Configuring Yourself

All config is in `~/.clawq/config.json` (or `$CLAWQ_HOME/config.json`). Modify via CLI or edit the file directly. After manual edits, validate with `clawq doctor`.

### Live config reload

The daemon automatically detects changes to `~/.clawq/config.json` by polling the file's mtime every 10 seconds. When a change is detected, the config is reloaded and propagated to all active sessions and agents. No restart is required for most config changes (provider settings, agent defaults, memory limits, heartbeat timing, security flags, etc.).

Manual reload: send `SIGHUP` to the daemon process (`kill -HUP <pid>`) to force an immediate config reload. The daemon logs "Config reloaded successfully" on success or "Config reload failed: ..." on error.

Note: Structural changes that affect daemon startup (enabling/disabling channels, changing gateway port/host, adding new channel credentials) require a daemon restart (`SIGUSR1` for graceful restart, or stop and start).

### Config set/get examples

```
clawq config set providers.openrouter.api_key "sk-or-v1-..."
clawq config set providers.openrouter.base_url "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"
clawq config set providers.openrouter.default_model "openai/gpt-4o"
clawq config set agent_defaults.primary_model "openrouter:openai/gpt-4o"
clawq config set providers.kimi_coding.api_key "sk-kimi-..."
clawq config set providers.kimi_coding.default_model "kimi-for-coding"
clawq config set agent_defaults.primary_model "kimi_coding:kimi-for-coding"
clawq config set agent_defaults.max_tool_iterations 10
clawq config set agent_defaults.reasoning_effort "medium"
clawq config set agent_defaults.show_thinking true
clawq config set agent_defaults.show_tool_calls true
clawq config set agent_defaults.autonomous_continuation_delay 90.0
clawq config set agent_defaults.autonomous_continuation_enabled false
clawq config set agent_defaults.max_concurrent_native_agents 3
clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.bot_token "123:ABC..."
clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.allow_from '["*"]'
clawq config set channels.discord.bot_token "MTIz..."
clawq config set channels.discord.allow_guilds '["*"]'
clawq config set channels.discord.allow_users '["*"]'
clawq config set channels.slack.bot_token "xoxb-..."
clawq config set channels.slack.app_token "xapp-..."
clawq config set channels.slack.socket_mode true
clawq config set channels.slack.signing_secret "abc123..."
clawq config set security.tools_enabled true
clawq config set security.workspace_only true
clawq config set security.audit_enabled true
clawq config set security.encrypt_secrets false
clawq config set security.audit_signing_enabled false
clawq config set security.landlock_enabled false
clawq config set security.sandbox_backend "auto"
clawq config set security.rate_limit.gateway_per_ip_rpm 60
clawq config set security.rate_limit.gateway_per_session_rpm 30
clawq config set security.rate_limit.telegram_per_chat_rpm 20
clawq config set security.rate_limit.burst_multiplier 1.5
clawq config set security.audit_retention.max_age_days 90
clawq config set security.audit_retention.max_entries 100000
clawq config set security.audit_retention.export_before_purge false
clawq config set gateway.host "127.0.0.1"
clawq config set gateway.port 13451
clawq config set gateway.require_pairing true
clawq config set memory.search_enabled true
clawq config set memory.max_messages_per_session 500
clawq config set memory.max_message_age_days 30
clawq config set memory.compaction_threshold_percent 80
clawq config set model_context_limits.glm-5.2 272000
clawq config set resilience.timeout_s 120.0
clawq config set resilience.max_retries 2
clawq config set resilience.base_delay_s 1.0
clawq config set resilience.fallback_provider "groq"
clawq config set mcp.enabled true
clawq config set tunnel.provider "cloudflare"
clawq config set tunnel.enabled false

clawq config get providers.openrouter.default_model
clawq config show
clawq config show channels
clawq config show security
```

### Model format (`provider:model`)

The canonical format for model references in config values (e.g. `agent_defaults.primary_model`) and commands (e.g. `/model set`, `/model set-default`, `models set-default`) is `provider:model` with a colon separator. Examples: `openai:gpt-5.4`, `anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`, `groq:llama-4-scout-17b-16e`.

The legacy `provider/model` slash format is still accepted but deprecated. Deprecation warnings appear in the following contexts:

- **Config load**: a warning is printed to stderr when `primary_model` uses the legacy format.
- **`status` command**: a warning is shown when the effective model uses the legacy format.
- **`config set agent_defaults.primary_model`**: a warning is shown if the value uses the legacy format.
- **`/model set`**: accepts both formats but prints a format hint when the legacy format is used.
- **`models set-default` / `/model set-default`**: auto-normalizes the legacy format to canonical before writing to config. Bare model names are accepted only when they resolve to one catalog entry; ambiguous bare names print candidate `provider:model` values.

Note: `providers.*.default_model` is the model identifier as understood by that provider's API (e.g. `openai/gpt-4o` on OpenRouter). This is a provider-specific model name, not a `provider:model` reference, and is not subject to the format deprecation.

Dynamic provider model discovery stores rows in `models_cache`, seeded from the catalog and refreshed from provider `/models` endpoints where supported. Cached rows have `deprecated` and `unavailable` flags; either flag makes a model unavailable for selection, and list surfaces hide unavailable models by default unless `availability=all` or an unavailable filter is requested.

### Model resolution hierarchy

When the agent selects a model for a turn, the following priority order applies (highest to lowest):

1. **Per-session model override** â€” set via `clawq session model SESSION set MODEL` or `/model set NAME`
2. **Channel-specific default model** â€” set via `clawq channel set-model CHANNEL MODEL`
3. **Global primary_model** â€” set via `agent_defaults.primary_model` in config

Native/local subagents use a narrower task-level hierarchy before falling back to the parent/global model: explicit task `--model`, then the agent template `model`, then `agent_defaults.subagent_default_model`, then the parent/global default. `agent_defaults.max_concurrent_native_agents` optionally caps concurrently running native/local subagents; queued native subagents wait for capacity.

Use `/model set-force <name>` to bypass model name validation when setting custom or local models not in the catalog.

### Config sections reference

**providers** â€” keyed by provider name. Fields: `api_key` (required for API-key providers; subscription/OAuth providers such as `openai-codex` can authenticate with provider-specific credentials), `base_url`, `kind`, `default_model`, `thinking_budget_tokens`, `oai_thinking_style` (default "none"), `project_id` (Vertex AI), `location` (Vertex AI), `service_account_json` (Vertex AI), `codex_oauth` (OAuth credentials object). The active provider is selected via `agent_defaults.primary_model` using the `provider:model` format. (`default_provider` at the top level is deprecated â€” see agent_defaults.) Provider names with built-in default base URLs: `openrouter` (fallback default), `mistral`, `xai`/`x_ai`, `deepseek`, `cohere`, `zai`, `zai_coding`, `kimi` (â†’ api.moonshot.cn/v1), `moonshot` (â†’ api.moonshot.cn/v1), `kimi_coding`/`kimi-code` (â†’ api.kimi.com/coding/v1), `minimax` (â†’ api.minimax.io; Anthropic-compatible requests use `/anthropic/v1/messages`), `xiaomi` (â†’ api.xiaomimimo.com/v1), `xiaomi-token-plan-cn` (â†’ token-plan-cn.xiaomimimo.com/v1), `xiaomi-token-plan-ams` (â†’ token-plan-ams.xiaomimimo.com/v1), `xiaomi-token-plan-sgp` (â†’ token-plan-sgp.xiaomimimo.com/v1). Kimi Coding subscription keys use the `sk-kimi-...` prefix; configure with provider name `kimi_coding` and the key will auto-route to the correct endpoint. The Xiaomi MiMo providers (`kind: "xiaomi"`, OpenAI-compatible, `oai_thinking_style: "reasoning_content"`) are zero-config: if a key is found in `XIAOMI_API_KEY`, `XIAOMI_TOKEN_PLAN_CN_API_KEY`, `XIAOMI_TOKEN_PLAN_AMS_API_KEY`, or `XIAOMI_TOKEN_PLAN_SGP_API_KEY` (the SGP provider also reads `~/.mimo`), the provider is synthesized in memory and never written to `config.json`. Models: public `xiaomi` offers `mimo-v2-flash`, `mimo-v2-omni`, `mimo-v2-pro`, `mimo-v2.5`, `mimo-v2.5-pro`, `mimo-v2.5-pro-ultraspeed`; the token-plan providers offer the same set minus `mimo-v2-flash`.

**model_context_limits** â€” optional top-level object mapping model names to manual token limits used for runtime context budgeting and history compaction. Keys may include provider prefixes (for example `openai-codex/gpt-5.4`). These values are not inferred or backfilled automatically; only explicitly configured entries are written. Lookup is normalized the same way model selection is normalized (case-insensitive, provider prefix/date suffix tolerated). Built-in defaults still exist for some known models; configured entries take precedence. Example: use `"openai-codex/gpt-5.4": 272000` to treat 272k as the practical soft cap for compaction even if the upstream provider allows a larger absolute window.

**agent_defaults** â€” `primary_model` (default `"openai-codex:gpt-5.4"`; canonical format is `provider:model`, e.g. `"anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6"`; the legacy `provider/model` slash format is accepted but triggers a deprecation warning on config load and in `status` output), `subagent_default_model` (optional `provider:model` default used by native/local subagents when no task or template model is set), `max_concurrent_native_agents` (optional positive integer cap for concurrently running native/local subagents), `system_prompt` (default ""), `max_tool_iterations` (default 10), `tool_search_enabled` (default false), `reasoning_effort` (optional string), `show_thinking` (default false), `show_tool_calls` (default true for connector notifiers), `send_continuation_checkin` (default false; when true, the "[automatic continuation check-in]" message is sent to connector notifiers before autonomous continuation prompts), `autonomous_continuation_delay` (default 90.0; seconds to wait before sending the autonomous continuation prompt after each turn), `autonomous_continuation_enabled` (default true; set to false to disable the periodic autonomous continuation check-in that prompts the agent to continue or go idle after each turn).

**prompt** â€” `dynamic_enabled` (default true), `include_tools_section` (default true), `include_safety_section` (default true), `include_workspace_section` (default true), `include_runtime_section` (default true), `include_datetime_section` (default true), `workspace_files` (default: AGENTS.md, EGO.md, SOUL.md, TOOLS.md, IDENTITY.md, USER.md, HEARTBEAT.md, BOOTSTRAP.md, MEMORY.md, memory.md), `max_workspace_file_chars` (default 8000), `max_workspace_total_chars` (default 20000). When `include_runtime_section` is enabled, the per-turn runtime block can include current cwd, effective workspace, git root/branch, OS, session id, optional session name, main-session status, whether heartbeat routing currently applies, `workspace_only`, requested/effective sandbox backend, shell policy and visible roots summary, plus estimated context-usage and compaction-threshold information.

**channels.telegram** â€” `text_coalesce_ms` (default 150, set to 0 to disable text coalescing). Telegram Bot API polling uses a 30s long-poll window with a 45s HTTP client timeout to avoid false local timeouts during normal long-poll waits. Under `accounts.<name>`: `bot_token`, `allow_from` (array of chat IDs or `["*"]`), `totp.enabled`, `totp.secret`, `totp.session_ttl_hours`.

**channels.discord** â€” `bot_token`, `allow_guilds` (default `["*"]`), `allow_users` (default `["*"]`), `intents`. Enabled by presence in config.

**channels.slack** â€” `bot_token`, `app_token` (for socket mode), `signing_secret` (for events API), `socket_mode` (bool, default false), `events_path` (default "/slack/events"), `allow_channels`, `allow_users`. Enabled by presence in config.

**channels.github** â€” `auth` (PAT or GitHub App installation token; see GitHub App Integration below), `repos` (array of repo configs with `name`, `webhook_secret`, `webhook_path`, `agent_name`, `allow_users`, `react_to`, `include_pr_files`). Session keys: `github:{owner}/{repo}:pr:{n}` for PR events/comments, `github:{owner}/{repo}:issue:{n}` for issue comments. Repeated `/clawq` interactions on the same thread resume the same session. Bare `/clawq <request>` runs an inline session turn; leading `runner=<auto|codex|claude>` and/or `host=<direct|herdr|tmux>` tokens route through a durable work-item envelope (github_work_items table): deduplicated on repo#issue:comment so webhook redelivery cannot launch twice, acknowledged as "Queued as work item N...", executed as a background task on the chosen runner/session host, ack comment edited into the final answer, states queued/running/blocked/succeeded/failed/cancelled, daemon-restart recovery re-publishes unpublished results. Repo config may set `local_repo_path` (local checkout): code-changing work items then run in an isolated worktree of it under the repository-owned policy `.clawq/publication-policy.json` (base_branch, publication reply_only|draft_pr, branch_prefix default "clawq/", allow_rebase/force_push/direct_merge/automerge, forbidden_commands/labels, validation; missing file = legacy-compatible default, malformed = error). The trusted publisher validates results: no commits -> reply only; commits -> push deterministic branch clawq/wi-<id> and open exactly one draft PR (idempotent) linked to the issue; dirty worktree or publication failure -> explicit recoverable failure reply. Base branch resolves policy > origin HEAD > GitHub API, never hardcoded. Intake triggers (B773): `/clawq`, a configured leading at-mention (`channels.github.trigger_login` â€” only the first non-blank, non-quoted line may address the agent; only the prefix is stripped), assignment of an issue to that identity, and an optional fallback label (`channels.github.trigger_label`, for identities that are not assignable) all normalize into the same authorized, deduplicated work-item path; quoted mentions, mid-text mentions, edits, bot self-replies, and assignment/labels of other identities never trigger. Remote workers (B774): the gateway exposes /worker/claim, /worker/heartbeat, /worker/complete, /worker/release, /worker/status (gateway auth token required; workers connect outbound only). Claims are atomic single-row leases with expiry and attempt limits; heartbeats and completion are lease-token-gated (stale tokens cannot revive or complete an item; same-token redelivery is an idempotent duplicate); the control plane reclaims expired leases every 30s and publishes remotely completed results idempotently. Provider subscription credentials never leave the worker; only result summaries are sent back. Comment lifecycle: acknowledge with eyes reaction, post placeholder comment (for issue/PR comments), process asynchronously, edit placeholder with final response. All bot replies include a `<!-- clawq-reply -->` marker for self-loop protection. Duplicate deliveries are suppressed via an LRU set keyed by X-GitHub-Delivery header. `allow_users` gates all webhook events except a small explicit system-event subset (`workflow_job`, `workflow_run`, `check_run`, `check_suite`, `ping`), so events with arbitrary user-controlled text such as `push` remain allowlisted. Hook payload snapshots are written on every verified delivery under `~/.clawq/workspace/tmp/github-deliveries/`, cleaned up opportunistically, and exposed to the agent via `payload_path` plus inline fallback JSON. File-backed event hooks from `~/.clawq/workspace/gh-hooks/` require explicit `repo` and `event` frontmatter and use exact-match `match:` fields. The webhook path must be unique per repo â€” ambiguous bindings return `409`.

**channels.whatsapp** â€” `phone_number_id`, `access_token`, `verify_token`, `allow_from`.

**channels.line** â€” `channel_access_token`, `channel_secret`, `allow_from`.

**channels.lark** â€” `enabled`, `app_id`, `app_secret`, `verification_token`, `endpoint`, `mode`, `allow_users`.

**channels.mattermost** â€” `url`, `access_token`, `team_id`, `channel_ids`, `allow_users`.

**channels.signal** â€” `base_url`, `account`, `api_mode`, `allow_from`, `max_chunk_bytes`.

**channels.matrix** â€” `homeserver_url`, `access_token`, `user_id`, `allow_rooms`, `allow_users`. Matrix `/sync` uses a 30s long-poll window with a 45s HTTP client timeout to avoid false local timeouts during normal sync waits.

**channels.irc** â€” `host`, `port`, `tls`, `nick`, `password`, `sasl`, `channels`, `allow_from`.

**channels.email** â€” `imap_host`, `imap_port`, `smtp_host`, `smtp_port`, `username`, `password`, `from_address`, `allow_from`, `poll_interval_s`.

**channels.nostr** â€” `relays`, `private_key`, `pubkey`, `nak_path`, `allow_from`.

**channels.dingtalk** â€” `app_key`, `app_secret`, `agent_id`, `allow_from`, `webhook_url`.

**channels.imessage** â€” `poll_interval_s`, `allow_from`.

**channels.onebot** â€” `ws_url`, `http_url`, `access_token`, `allow_from`, `allow_groups`.

**channels.teams** â€” `app_id`, `app_secret`, `tenant_id`, `webhook_path` (default `/teams/webhook`), `service_url` (default `https://smba.trafficmanager.net/amer`), `allow_teams` (default `["*"]`), `allow_users` (default `["*"]`).

**security** â€” `workspace_only` (default true), `audit_enabled` (default false), `tools_enabled` (default true), `encrypt_secrets` (default false), `audit_signing_enabled` (default false), `landlock_enabled` (default false), `landlock_extra_read_paths` (default []), `extra_allowed_paths` (default []), `sandbox_backend` (default "auto": auto-detects firejail > bubblewrap > none), `hosted_runner_isolation` (default "off"; "prefer" sandboxes hosted codex/claude background runners when a backend exists, "require" fails task startup closed without one â€” remote workers must use "require"; applies a minimal allowlisted environment stripping GitHub/SSH/cloud/API-key credentials plus an argv-level bwrap/firejail wrapper limiting the runner to its worktree, log dir, and provider auth dirs; see docs/hosted-runner-isolation.md), `allowed_cwd_patterns` (default `["$CLAWQ_WORKSPACE/**", "$USER_HOME/src/projects-clawq/**", "/clawq/path/to/somewhere/else/**"]`; glob patterns for directories agents may `change_working_dir` into; supports `$CLAWQ_WORKSPACE` and `$USER_HOME` pseudo-variable expansion).

**security.rate_limit** â€” `gateway_per_ip_rpm` (default 60), `gateway_per_session_rpm` (default 30), `telegram_per_chat_rpm` (default 20), `burst_multiplier` (default 1.5).

**security.audit_retention** â€” `max_age_days` (default 90), `max_entries` (default 100000), `export_before_purge` (default false), `export_path` (default ~/.clawq/audit_exports).

**gateway** â€” `host` (default 127.0.0.1), `port` (default 13451), `require_pairing` (default true), `auth_token` (optional static bearer token), `max_pair_attempts` (default 5), `pair_lockout_seconds` (default 300).

**memory** â€” `backend` (default "sqlite"), `search_enabled` (default false), `db_path` (default empty string; effective runtime path is `~/.clawq/memory.db` when unset), `vector_weight` (default 50), `keyword_weight` (default 50), `embedding_provider`, `embedding_model`, `compaction_threshold_percent` (default 80; valid range 1-99), `max_messages_per_session` (default 500), `max_message_age_days` (default 30).

**resilience** â€” `timeout_s` (default 120.0), `max_retries` (default 2), `base_delay_s` (default 1.0), `fallback_provider` (optional).

**mcp** â€” `enabled` (default true), `exposed_tools` (optional array of tool names; omit for all), `runner_relay_enabled` (default true; enables HTTP endpoints `/mcp`, `/runner/ask`, `/runner/token` for remote runner question relay), `runner_token_ttl_hours` (default 24), `runner_question_timeout_s` (default 300). External MCP clients are loaded from `~/.clawq/mcp_servers.json`; each entry can be either local stdio (`name`, `command`, optional `args`/`env`) or a remote JSON-RPC-over-HTTP endpoint (`name`, `url`, optional `headers`).

**tunnel** â€” `provider` (default "cloudflare"; supports cloudflare, tailscale, ngrok, custom), `enabled` (default false), `url`, `managed`, `tunnel_name`, `config_dir`. Live reconfiguration: the daemon detects tunnel config changes on SIGHUP and config file changes (10s poll), and will start/stop/restart the tunnel accordingly. Use `tunnel apply` to trigger reconfiguration explicitly. Managed/named Cloudflare tunnels require pre-existing `cloudflared tunnel create` and DNS setup outside clawq.

**runtime** â€” `docker_image` (default "clawq:latest"), `docker_container_name` (default "clawq"), `docker_port` (default 13451).

**stt** â€” `provider`, `model`, `language` (optional). When configured, enables automatic voice transcription across all channels (Telegram, Discord, Slack, Teams) with progress messages and validation limits (25 MB, 1 hour, music detection).

**voice** â€” `stt_enabled`, `tts_enabled`, `stt_provider`, `tts_provider`, `tts_model`, `tts_voice`, `audio_dir`.

**web_channel** â€” `enabled`, `path_prefix` (default "/web"), `totp_secret` (optional), `token_ttl_hours` (default 24).

**telemetry** â€” `enabled`, `endpoint`, `service_name`.

**heartbeat** â€” `enabled` (default true), `interval_seconds` (default 250), `quiet_start` (default 23), `quiet_end` (default 8).

**notify** â€” `notify_channel`, `notify_target`. Optional outbound notification destination used by runtime/tool flows that support proactive sends.

**web_search** â€” `search_provider` ("brave" or "ddg"), `search_api_key`, `num_results`, `search_base_url` (optional, for SearXNG override). When the daemon is running, config reload updates the active runtime config object, so changes here are intended to take effect without a full restart.

**zai_mcp** â€” Enables Z.ai MCP-powered web tools. Fields: `enabled` (bool, default true when section present), `api_key` (optional; auto-detected from `providers.zai.api_key` or `providers.zai_coding.api_key` if absent), `websearch_enabled` (bool, default true), `webfetch_enabled` (bool, default true). When configured, registers `zai_websearch` and `zai_webfetch` tools. Auth uses Z.ai GLM Coding Plan API key as Bearer token.

**agent_bindings** â€” array of routing bindings for dispatching messages to specific agents by pattern.

**log** â€” daemon log rotation. `max_size_mb` (default 10), `max_files` (default 5). When the daemon log exceeds `max_size_mb`, it is rotated; up to `max_files` historical log files are retained.

**error_watcher** â€” error correction watcher configuration. `ec_enabled` (default: true only for `-dev` builds), `scan_interval_s` (default 30.0), `primary_models` (default `["anthropic:claude-opus-4-6", "openai-codex:gpt-5.4"]`), `fallback_models` (default `["zai_coding:glm-5", "kimi_coding:kimi-for-code"]`), `cooldown_s` (default 300.0), `max_errors_per_batch` (default 10), `ignore_patterns` (default []), `auto_fix_enabled` (default false), `ec_commit_tag` (default `"[INTERNAL_EC]"`).

**observer** â€” turn observer for detecting stuck/looping agent behavior. `enabled` (default true), `model` (default `"groq:openai/gpt-oss-120b"`), `check_every_n_messages` (default 5), `round1_window` (default 8), `round2_window` (default 30), `thinking_token_threshold` (default 5000), `consecutive_errors_threshold` (default 3), `repeat_call_threshold` (default 2).

**summarizer** â€” autosummarizer for long tool output. `enabled` (default true), `model` (default `"groq:openai/gpt-oss-120b"`), `escalation_model` (optional), `threshold_chars` (default 1500), `p1_max_chars` (default 200000), `p2_max_chars` (default 12000), `context_window_messages` (default 4), `excluded_tools` (default `["tool_search", "unsummarize", "summarize_thread", "thread_summary"]`), `max_age_days` (default 30), `envelope_template` (optional). Changes take effect live via daemon file watcher or SIGHUP (no restart needed). Legacy keys `summarizer_enabled` and `summarizer_model` are accepted for backwards compatibility.

### Secret encryption

To encrypt API keys at rest: set `security.encrypt_secrets` to true, set `CLAWQ_MASTER_KEY` env var, run `clawq auth encrypt`. Keys become `$ENC:...` and auto-decrypt at runtime.

## Your Tools

Built-in tool availability depends on runtime configuration and wiring. Core help, file, shell, HTTP, browser, git, model, usage, document, and background-shell tools are normally registered; web search, Z.ai web tools, send/poll/file tools, transcription, memory/history tools, task-tree tools, native subagent tools, `background_finalize`, plan-pipeline tools, connector-history injection, and debate are registered only when their required runtime dependencies are present.

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `shell_exec` | Execute a shell command (37-command allowlist when workspace_only, no pipes/semicolons/redirects/subshells; optional `cwd`, `head`/`tail` line windows on final stdout/stderr result, `background=true` for immediate background-shell handoff, timeout 30s default, 600s max). On user interrupt, the process is also moved to a background shell instead of being killed - use `bg_shell_status`/`bg_shell_wait`/`bg_shell_result` to track it |
| `file_read` | Read a file's text content (50K char limit; use offset/limit for larger files) |
| `file_write` | Create or overwrite a file with the given content |
| `file_append` | Append content to the end of a file, creating it if it does not exist |
| `file_edit` | Edit a file by replacing a string match |
| `file_edit_lines` | Replace an inclusive 1-indexed line range with new content |
| `glob` | Find files matching a glob pattern (* and ** wildcards). Returns absolute paths |
| `list_dir` | List directory contents with type labels (file/dir) for each entry |
| `grep` | Search files for a regex pattern (OCaml Str syntax, supports \| alternation). Returns matching lines with path and line number |
| `http_get` | Fetch a URL via GET, return raw body (10KB truncation; localhost-only when workspace_only). For HTML use web_fetch |
| `http_request` | HTTP request with configurable method/headers/body (20KB truncation; localhost-only when workspace_only). For web pages use web_fetch |
| `web_fetch` | Fetch a URL, return readable text with HTML stripped (20KB truncation; localhost-only when workspace_only). For raw API responses use http_get/http_request |
| `web_search` | Search the web (conditional: only if web_search config is set) |
| `zai_websearch` | Search the web via Z.ai's webSearchPrime MCP tool (conditional: only if zai_mcp config is set and websearch_enabled) |
| `zai_webfetch` | Fetch a webpage via Z.ai's webReader MCP tool, returning title, main content, and links (conditional: only if zai_mcp config is set and webfetch_enabled) |
| `clawq_help` | Get concise built-in Clawq help. Omit `topic` or use `general` to list topics; focused topics include tools, channels, config, skills, agents, memory, background-tasks, subagents, security, models, web-ui, crons, git-worktrees, and onboarding |
| `git_operations` | Git operations: status, diff, log, branch, add, commit, checkout, stash, show |
| `send_message` | Send a message to the user via the current session or notification channel. Use when asked to notify/alert the user (conditional: runtime wiring) |
| `send_poll` | Send a poll with inline buttons (conditional: runtime wiring) |
| `ask_user_question` | Ask the user one or more clarifying questions and wait for answers. Questions are sent sequentially; each blocks until answered. Supports types: single_select, multi_select, text, number, confirm, rating, file_upload, date. Returns JSON array of {question, answer, notes?}. Rendering adapts per connector: Telegram gets inline keyboard buttons (single_select, confirm, rating â‰¤5) and native polls (multi_select); Teams gets Adaptive Cards with Action.Submit buttons; other connectors get Format_adapter-escaped formatted text via Content_dsl. Users can always reply with typed text instead of clicking buttons. Only available in interactive channel sessions (Telegram, Discord, Slack, Teams, web) (conditional: runtime wiring) |
| `doc_write` | Write or update a workspace document |
| `transcribe` | Transcribe an audio file |
| `memory_store` | Store a persistent key-value memory that survives across sessions (overwrites on key collision) |
| `memory_recall` | Search persistent memories by full-text query, return matching key-content pairs |
| `memory_forget` | Remove a persistent memory by its exact key and archive the forgotten content in the session workspace |
| `memory_list` | List all persistent memories, optionally filtered by category |
| `compact_history` | Compact (summarize) older conversation history to free up context window space. Useful for clearing out old patterns or lots of junk data, and should be done proactively. The agent will be forked and the clone asked to save everything necessary to memory, so compaction can safely be run at any time without fear of losing unremembered things. Returns token usage before and after compaction. |
| `history_search` | Search your own session's chat history across current and archived epochs |
| `task_start_agent` | Start one `task_tree` task as a native/local subagent using that task's `agent_prompt`, optional `agent_model`, and optional `agent_type`; records the queued background task id back on the task |
| `subagent` | Spawn a native/local subagent task backed by background-task infrastructure |
| `subagent_result` | Read or wait for a native/local subagent result. With `wait=true`, wait in prompt-cache-aware intervals and return early if queued steering or interruption needs parent-session attention |
| `bg_shell_status` | Check the status of a background shell job (created by `shell_exec` with `background=true` or when `shell_exec` is interrupted). Returns status, elapsed time, and last 20 lines of log |
| `bg_shell_wait` | Wait for a background shell job to complete (max 110s). Returns job result on completion, or timeout/interrupt message |
| `bg_shell_result` | Get the result and log output of a completed background shell job. Supports `head`/`tail` line windowing |
| `background_finalize` | Finalize a completed background task by rebasing its worktree branch onto the target branch, fast-forward merging, and cleaning up the worktree. Refuses to proceed if the worktree has uncommitted changes (staged or unstaged). Use after automerge fails or when manually finalizing |
| `task_tree` | Track and organize work using a persistent hierarchical task tree. Survives context compaction, visible every turn. Operations: add, update, remove, clear, archive, plus agent-ready task metadata. Statuses: pending, in_progress, done, error, cancelled. Task metadata includes `depends_on`, `agent_model`, `agent_type`, `agent_prompt`, `agent_details`, and `autostart`. Supports depth-based auto-nesting, dependency/lifecycle enforcement (parent can't be done until children are), archiving completed subtrees, and autostarting ready tasks as native/local subagents when dependencies are satisfied |
| `plan_pipeline_start` | Start a multi-stage planning pipeline (foreground/blocking): planner agent writes `plan.md`, plan-review loop refines it, coder agent implements, code-review loop verifies. Params: `prompt` (required), `repo_path`, `runner`, `planner_model`, `reviewer_model`, `coder_model`, `max_plan_review_iters` (default 3), `max_code_review_iters` (default 3). Set iters to 0 to skip a review phase |
| `plan_pipeline_status` | Get the current stage and status of a pipeline by id |
| `plan_pipeline_list` | List all planning pipelines with id, stage, status, and prompt summary |
| `plan_pipeline_logs` | Read log output from the most recent background task for a pipeline. Params: `id` (required), `lines` (default 50) |
| `plan_pipeline_cancel` | Cancel a running pipeline and its current background task |
| `change_working_dir` | Change the agent's effective working directory. Relative paths resolve from the current effective CWD. Optional `wipe_history` (bool, default false) compacts history to first user message + mechanical summary. Restricted by `security.allowed_cwd_patterns` glob patterns. All path-resolving tools (file_read/write/edit, glob, list_dir, grep, shell_exec) use the effective CWD for relative path resolution while security checks still use the original workspace |

Background delegation notes:
- `subagent` and `subagent_result` are the LLM-facing tool surface for native/local subagents.
- CLI slash commands and `clawq background` commands remain the lower-level control surface: queue work, inspect task metadata, wait for completion, read captured logs, resume a prior run, inject follow-up task messages, cancel a run, or finalize (rebase and fast-forward merge) a completed task.
- Background task worktrees live under `~/.clawq/background-worktrees/`.
- Background task logs live under `~/.clawq/background-logs/`.
- Background shell job logs (from explicit `background=true` or interrupted `shell_exec`) live under `~/.clawq/bg-shells/`.
- Automerge: background task CLI flows can use `automerge` (bool, default true) and `use_worktree` (bool, default true). When `automerge` is true and the task succeeds, the daemon automatically rebases the task branch onto the target branch, fast-forwards the target branch (via `git merge --ff-only` when the target is checked out, or `git update-ref` otherwise), and cleans up the worktree. If a conflict occurs, the task is marked and the parent agent is notified to use `background_finalize` to retry. If the worktree has uncommitted changes (staged or unstaged), automerge and `background_finalize` refuse to proceed and report the dirty state â€” commit or discard changes first. `use_worktree=false` runs the agent directly in the repo directory without worktree isolation (automerge is ignored).
- Resume/message flow: `background resume`, `background message`, `background send`, and `subagents send` work for tasks that already started. External runners track their session ID automatically: Claude pre-assigns a UUID via `--session-id`, Codex parses JSONL `thread_id` from `--json` output, OpenCode/Kimi/Cursor extract session IDs from log output via regex, and Gemini uses index-based resume (no specific ID). Local tasks resume the stable `__bg_task:<id>` session. Message delivery is durable and replayed FIFO into the resumed runner invocation. Use `subagents transcript <id>` for bounded transcript access; regex filtering is applied before the 200-line default and 300-line hard cap, and oversized results are exported as JSONL instead of injected inline.
- Runner question relay: when a background task is spawned, the daemon auto-sets `CLAWQ_RUNNER_TOKEN`, `CLAWQ_MCP_URL`, and `CLAWQ_RUNNER_ASK_URL` env vars in the runner process. Runners can use the MCP endpoint (standard `tools/list` + `tools/call`) or the REST endpoint (`POST /runner/ask` with `{"questions":[...]}`) to relay questions back to the user's active channel. Tokens are localhost-only, SHA256-hashed in memory, with configurable TTL (default 24h). Manual tokens: `clawq runner token --session KEY`.
- Session routing preservation: when `shell_exec` runs inside an active session (Telegram, Discord, etc.), it injects the `CLAWQ_SESSION_ID` env var into the child process. CLI subprocesses (e.g. `clawq background add`) read this variable to capture the originating session's routing metadata (session_key, channel, channel_id) on the task row, enabling completion notices to return to the originating channel. Standalone CLI launches without this env var fall back to static `notify` config or no routing.
- Completion notifications: when a background task finishes, a concise channel-friendly message is dispatched to the originating channel/session. Format: uppercase status (SUCCEEDED/FAILED/DIRTY WORKTREE/CANCELLED), runner + repo basename + elapsed time, optional git status (dirty/clean, commit count, rebased), LLM-generated summary (with 5s timeout, falls back to truncated result_preview), and actionable hints for failures (`background retry`/`background logs`) or dirty worktrees (`background finalize`). Notification delivery is tracked per-task (delivered/failed/skipped) with error details; visible in `background show`.

Inspection paging pattern:
- `session show` supports `--offset N` and `--limit N` for paging through messages. The JSON response includes `total_messages`, `has_more` (bool), and `next_offset` (int, present when `has_more` is true). Default behavior (no paging flags) returns all messages for backward compatibility.
- `background logs` supports `--offset LINE` and `--lines COUNT` for forward reading from a specific line number. The text output includes metadata lines: `total_lines`, `has_more`, and `next_offset`. Default behavior (no `--offset`) tails the last N lines. `--follow` streams live output and is mutually exclusive with `--offset`.
- Continuation workflow: issue a read with `--offset 0 --limit N` (or `--offset 0 --lines N`), check `has_more` and `next_offset` in the response, then issue a follow-up with `--offset <next_offset>` to continue. Repeat until `has_more` is false.

Security features:
- Dual path safety: Coq-extracted pure check (PathSafety.v) AND OCaml realpath check â€” both must pass
- 37-command shell allowlist: ls, cat, head, tail, grep, find, wc, sort, uniq, echo, pwd, date, whoami, which, file, stat, diff, patch, mkdir, touch, git, make, dune, opam, npm, yarn, jq, sed, awk, tr, cut, tee, tar, zip, unzip, gzip, gunzip
- Unsafe shell syntax rejection (Coq-verified + OCaml check)
- Localhost-only http_get/http_request/web_fetch when workspace_only is enabled
- Tool sandboxing via Firejail or Bubblewrap (auto-detected, configurable via security.sandbox_backend)
- Working directory restrictions: `change_working_dir` is gated by `security.allowed_cwd_patterns` glob list with `$CLAWQ_WORKSPACE`/`$USER_HOME` expansion

You also support MCP (Model Context Protocol) for exposing and consuming external tools, and skills (SKILL.md definitions with YAML frontmatter and shell-script extensions, discovered from `.claude-p/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`, and `~/.clawq/skills/`; managed via `clawq skills`). Skill frontmatter fields: `name` (optional â€” inferred from directory/filename if omitted), `description` (required), `allowed-tools`, `model`, `disable-model-invocation` (when true, excludes skill from model-accessible tool lists â€” only invocable by users via slash commands or @mention). Legacy JSON skills are deprecated; when loading `.json` skills, every `{{param_name}}` placeholder in `command` must be declared under `parameters.properties`, or clawq refuses the skill at load time and logs an actionable warning. No-argument skill invocation is context-aware: if the skill is already retained since session start or the last compaction, clawq skips reloading it and skips the load notification; if compaction removes it from retained context, the next invocation loads it again.

Agent templates are Markdown files with YAML frontmatter that define role-based personas with tool restrictions. Managed via `clawq agents`. Discovery order: `~/.clawq/agents/<name>.md` or `<name>/AGENT.md`, workspace `.claude-p/agents/` and `.claude/agents/`. User templates override builtins with the same name.

### agents

Manage agent templates â€” built-in archetypes and user-defined templates with role-based tool restrictions.

Subcommands:
- `agents list` â€” List all templates (builtins marked [builtin], user marked [user])
- `agents show <name>` â€” Full template details including system prompt preview
- `agents create <name>` â€” Create template .md in ~/.clawq/agents/
- `agents edit <name>` â€” Edit template (copies builtin to user dir first)
- `agents delete <name>` â€” Delete user template (refuses for builtins)
- `agents bind <pattern> <agent> [--priority N]` â€” Add routing binding to config.json
- `agents unbind <pattern>` â€” Remove routing binding
- `agents bindings` â€” List current agent bindings
- `agents setup` â€” Interactive setup wizard
- `agents path` â€” Show template search directories

Built-in agent templates (11):
- ceo: High-level strategy, delegation (read-only, no shell/write)
- team-lead: Task orchestration, progress tracking (shell + bg tasks)
- coder: General implementation (full tools)
- planner: Architecture and design (read-only + shell for exploration)
- reviewer: Code review (read + test, no write)
- researcher: Information gathering (read + http, no write/shell)
- tester: Test writing and execution (shell + read + write)
- debugger: Bug investigation and fixes (shell + read + edit)
- refactorer: Code cleanup (shell + read + write + edit)
- documenter: Documentation (read + write, no shell)
- ops: CI/CD and infrastructure (shell + read + write)

Agent template format: Markdown with YAML frontmatter. Fields: name, description, role, goal, backstory, model, max-tool-iterations, allowed-tools, disallowed-tools, tool-search-enabled, reasoning-effort.

Template discovery: ~/.clawq/agents/<name>.md or <name>/AGENT.md, workspace .claude-p/agents/ and .claude/agents/. User templates override builtins with the same name.

Agent dispatch surfaces:
- `/agent <name> <prompt>` â€” one-off agent template invocation from any channel
- `/agent menu [page]` â€” paginated template browser (8 per page, next/prev navigation)
- `/delegate @name <prompt>` â€” delegate with a specific agent's system prompt and tool restrictions
- `/fork_and @name <prompt>` â€” fork with agent tool restrictions (parent system prompt preserved for cache)
- `/bg create @name <prompt>` â€” background task using agent template
- `@name <prompt>` in chat messages â€” mention-based dispatch (after bot-name stripping)
- Agent router bindings (`agents bind`) â€” auto-apply agent templates to sessions by channel, user, or guild pattern

## Shell and Filesystem Execution Model

`file_*`, `glob`, `list_dir`, and `grep` are direct OCaml file operations in the daemon process. They do not run through a shell. `shell_exec` is different.

### `shell_exec` when `security.workspace_only = true` (default)

- Default cwd is the configured workspace directory (`clawq workspace`; usually `~/.clawq/workspace/`), not the repo you happen to be asking about.
- The tool accepts an optional `cwd` parameter. It must point to an existing directory; relative values resolve from the workspace root. In `workspace_only = true`, `cwd` must stay under the workspace or `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- The tool also accepts optional `head` and `tail` integer parameters. They affect only the final returned `stdout`/`stderr` text, not live streamed output chunks. If either causes lines to be omitted, clawq shows a head/tail window with an omission marker and saves the full stream to `~/.clawq/tool-output/` for later inspection.
- The command is validated before execution: unsafe shell syntax is rejected, the first token must be an allowed command, and absolute/`~` path arguments must stay under the workspace or `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- Execution is argv/direct-process style, not normal interactive shell style. In practice this means shell builtins such as `cd` and `command` are not available as standalone commands.
- `&&`, `||`, pipes, redirects, subshells, glob expansion by the shell, and similar compound shell syntax are rejected in `workspace_only` mode.
- `HOME` and `PATH` are set explicitly for the child process; existing common user binary directories (`~/.local/share/pnpm`, `~/.local/share/pnpm/bin`, `~/.cargo/bin`, `~/.bun/bin`, `~/.local/bin`, `~/.npm-global/bin`, and `~/.opam/*/bin`) are appended to `PATH`; other environment inheritance is intentionally minimal.
- Timeout defaults to 30 seconds. The LLM can pass a `timeout` parameter (in seconds, max 600) for longer-running commands.
- The LLM can pass `background=true` to return immediately with a background shell job ID and continue tracking output through `bg_shell_status`, `bg_shell_wait`, and `bg_shell_result`.
- Egress rules are not a shell network firewall. In default `workspace_only` mode the shell sandbox uses `--net=none` where supported; otherwise shell network behavior is governed by the sandbox/backend, not by HTTP egress rules.

### `shell_exec` when `security.workspace_only = false`

- Commands are executed via `Lwt_process.shell`, so normal `/bin/sh -c`-style shell semantics apply.
- Shell builtins and compound syntax generally work here.
- Optional `cwd` still works here and can be used to make worktree-targeting explicit instead of relying on persistent shell state.
- Commands starting with `cd <dir> &&` still run, but clawq warns the LLM to pass `cwd="<dir>"` and remove the `cd` prefix.
- Optional `head` and `tail` still apply to the final returned `stdout`/`stderr` text here, and omitted full streams are saved under `~/.clawq/tool-output/`.
- Filesystem isolation is not applied in this mode; shell sees the host filesystem like the file tools do.
- Egress rules still do not inspect arbitrary shell command network access in this mode.

### Sandbox backend and visible filesystem

- `security.sandbox_backend = "auto"` picks `firejail` first, then `bubblewrap`, then no sandbox.
- In `workspace_only = true`, Firejail wrapper: `firejail --private=<workspace> --whitelist=<extra_allowed_path>... --net=none --quiet --noprofile -- /bin/sh -c <cmd>`.
- In `workspace_only = true`, Bubblewrap wrapper: `bwrap --ro-bind /usr /usr --unshare-all --bind <workspace> <workspace> ... --bind <extra_allowed_path> <extra_allowed_path> ... --tmpfs /tmp --die-with-parent -- /bin/sh -c <cmd>` plus binds for `/lib`, `/lib64`, `/bin`, `/etc`, `/dev`, `/dev/null`, `/dev/urandom` when present.
- Existing common user binary directories (`~/.local/share/pnpm`, `~/.local/share/pnpm/bin`, `~/.cargo/bin`, `~/.bun/bin`, `~/.local/bin`, `~/.npm-global/bin`, and `~/.opam/*/bin`) are exposed automatically: Firejail whitelists them and Bubblewrap binds them read-only.
- File tools always use direct OCaml filesystem access with path checks.
- In `workspace_only = true`, shell and file tools are intended to agree on the allowed roots: workspace plus `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- In `workspace_only = false`, shell and file tools both use the host filesystem view.

### `grep` caveats

- `grep` uses OCaml filesystem walking, not shell `grep`.
- `path` defaults to the workspace root if omitted.
- `include` is an alias for `file_glob`; if both are present, `include` wins.
- The filename filter is matched only against the file basename, not the full relative path. `*.ml` works; patterns that expect directory segments do not.
