- The filter also applies when `path` points to a single file. If you pass `path: "src/runtime_config.ml"` with `include: "*.txt"`, the file is skipped and you will get "No matches found".
- `pattern` is treated as a regular expression. To match a literal `|`, escape it as `\|`; an unescaped `|` is alternation.
- If a file is unreadable or disappears during traversal, the tool silently skips it.

### Path and cwd diagnostics

- To see the workspace root used by tools: `clawq workspace`.
- To inspect current security settings: `clawq config show security`.
- To allow host paths outside the workspace for path-checked tools, add them to `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- `~/src` in a shell command is expanded before path safety checks. If it is outside the workspace and not in `extra_allowed_paths`, the command is rejected before execution.
- `clawq workspace` reports the configured effective workspace, not the process cwd.

## Session Identity and Routing

- Session keys identify independent conversation histories and locks.
- Web chat uses `web:<session_id>`.
- Telegram uses `telegram:<chat_id>:<user_id>`.
- Discord uses `discord:<channel_id>:<author_id>`.
- Slack uses `slack:<channel_id>:<user_id>`.
- The runtime-context block shown to the model always includes the current session id. Session name is optional metadata; today it is only set for special internal cases such as the main session or debug prompt flows.
- Registered channel notifiers exist only while a live channel request/stream is active; they are used for restart drain warnings and similar out-of-band messages to currently attached sessions.

## Durable Inbound Queue

Offline `session inject` writes to an SQLite `inbound_queue` table instead of
chat history.  Tracked fields: queue_id, session_key, message text, state
(pending/claimed/failed), attempt_count, last_error, created_at.  At-least-once
delivery: rows are claimed, processed through Session.turn, and deleted on
success; failures increment attempt_count and record last_error.  Replay is
FIFO per session (creation order).  At daemon startup the replay step runs
after pending agent session resume.  `session pending SESSION` shows the
current queue state for a given session; `session list` appends
`pending_inbound=N` when queued rows exist.

## Room Agents

Room agents bind a connector room (Slack channel, Discord channel, Teams
conversation, etc.) to a shared room profile with its own model, system prompt,
tool policy, workspace, budget, routines, and ambient follow-up policy.
Configured profiles live in `room_profiles`; active room bindings live in
`room_profile_bindings`.

- Shared room sessions use connector-specific room keys; thread work uses
  `__room_child_thread:*` keys; scheduled routines use
  `__room_routine:<profile_id>:<routine_id>` keys.
- `clawq rooms bind ROOM_ID PROFILE_ID --preserve|--reset` attaches a room to a
  profile. Mutations require `CLAWQ_ADMIN=1`.
- `clawq rooms memory list|show|save` manages room-scoped memories. Bound room
  profiles have implicit owner access; CLI calls reconcile config bindings
  before access checks. First saves create scopes for bound rooms or admins;
  unbound non-admin saves and stale/duplicate bindings fail closed.
- Room routines are cron-backed jobs managed through `clawq rooms routine ...`.
  They run under the room profile and can be triggered manually with
  `rooms routine trigger NAME`.
- Ambient follow-up is opt-in with `ambient_enabled: true`. The daemon checks
  stale background/task-tree work, applies quiet hours, rate limits, budget
  state, connector capability gates, and material-change suppression, then
  records delivery attempts in the room activity ledger.
- Supported ambient-history connectors are currently Slack, Discord, and Teams;
  unsupported connectors are policy-denied rather than delivered.
- Inspect operator state with `clawq rooms ledger ...` and the ambient inspection
  command surfaces. See `docs/src/content/docs/room-agents.mdx` for full
  operator guidance.

## Room Access Policy and Scope Bundles

Room profiles define access policy through `access_bundles` (permission sets) and `access_scopes` (layered overrides). This system controls tool access, codebase grants, repo grants, memory grants, and scoped egress rules. Top-level `egress` config supplies the fallback strictness/default allowlist for policy-aware HTTP when scoped rules do not match.

### Access Bundles

An access bundle (`access_bundles` in config) is a named permission set with:
- `allowed_tools` / `denied_tools` â€” tool allow/deny lists
- `codebase_grants` â€” filesystem path patterns the agent may access
- `repo_grants` â€” repository access with capabilities (`Read`, `Write`)
- `memory_grants` â€” cross-scope memory access grants
- `egress_rules` â€” network egress rules (allow/deny host/path/method patterns)

Bundles are referenced by ID from scopes and profiles. A missing bundle reference causes fail-closed behavior (zero grants from that scope/profile).

### Access Scopes

Scopes layer bundles with precedence. Four scope levels exist with fixed priority:

| Level | Rank | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| Default | 0 | System-wide defaults |
| Workspace | 1 | Workspace-level overrides |
| Channel | 2 | Channel-type overrides (e.g., all Slack rooms) |
| Room | 3 | Room-specific overrides (highest priority) |

Scopes are merged in rank order (ascending), then lexicographically by scope ID within the same level. Profile bundles (from `room_profiles`) are treated as the room layer and appended after scope bundles.

### Merge Rules

- **Deny wins over allow**: If a tool appears in `denied_tools` anywhere in merged bundles, it is removed from `allowed_tools`.
- **Same-bundle deny wins**: deny within a single bundle overrides allow in the same bundle.
- **Room deny overrides workspace allow**: Room-level deny takes precedence over workspace-level allow for the same tool.
- **Duplicate bundles**: References to the same bundle from multiple scopes merge provenance but do not duplicate values.
- **Declaration order preserved**: Within the same scope, bundles referenced by `access_bundle_ids` are merged in list order.

### Global Security Caps

When `security.workspace_only = true`, codebase grants outside the workspace (and `extra_allowed_paths`) are blocked. Inherited grants do not weaken global security. `allowed_cwd_patterns` provides an additional ceiling.

### Repo Grant Resolution

- Explicit `repo_grants` suppress legacy `repositories` for the same repo key.
- Repo grants are subject to global security checks.
- Repo grants require codebase grant coverage when `codebase_grants` is non-empty.
- Wildcard repo grants require exact codebase grant match (not glob).
- Legacy `repositories` become read-only (`[Read]`) repo grants.

### Effective Access Snapshots

An `Access_snapshot` captures the effective access for a session at a point in time. Snapshots are:
- **Immutable**: Once created, the record is not mutated.
- **Auditable**: Carry a `config_hash` (SHA-256 of serialized config) and full provenance (`bundle_sources` recording which bundle/layer/field contributed each grant).
- **Tool-gating**: `tool_denial` matches the resolver's allowed/denied lists exactly.
- **Persisted**: Written to DB for audit trail; reloaded on restart.

### Channel Isolation

Room scopes with a `channel` selector only match sessions from that channel type. Missing layer selectors are not wildcards â€” a workspace scope without a `workspace` field matches nothing.

### Config Reload

Config reload re-parses from disk, picking up changes to bundles, scopes, and profiles. Existing room profiles survive reload intact. Invalid JSON does not crash the runtime. Tool access decisions are stable across reload when config is unchanged. Malformed access policy triggers fail-closed.

## Room Memory

Room-scoped memory provides per-room persistent storage with visibility controls, grant-based cross-scope access, and audit logging.

### CRUD Operations

- **save** (`rooms memory save ROOM_ID REFERENCE CONTENT`) â€” create or update a memory; first saves create scopes for bound rooms or admins
- **list** (`rooms memory list ROOM_ID`) â€” list memories the room can see
- **show** (`rooms memory show ROOM_ID MEMORY_ID`) â€” show a specific memory
- **correct** â€” update memory content (via tool)
- **forget** â€” redact a memory (sets content to NULL, archives to workspace)

All CRUD operations are gated by `check_room_access` which requires either a profile binding (ownership) or an explicit grant. Raw API calls (`query_scoped_memories`, `correct_scoped_memory`, `delete_scoped_memory`) do not enforce scope isolation by themselves â€” isolation is enforced at the tool layer.

### Visibility Levels

Three levels: `Public` (default, visible to all in scope), `Private` (visible only to scope owner), `Team` (visible only to principals with an explicit team grant via `memory_team_grants` table). Team grants do not override scope isolation. Visibility is preserved on upsert when not explicitly provided.

### Grants

Memory grants are direct, not transitive. Grant mutations require admin privileges. Expired grants (`expires_at` in the past) and revoked grants (`revoked_at IS NULL`) are excluded from resolution.

### Redaction (Forgetting)

- Redaction is idempotent; redacted memories cannot be corrected.
- Redacted memories are excluded from `query_scoped_memories` (list, content-search).
- Redaction clears content to NULL, sets `redacted_at` timestamp, scrubs provenance of `prev_content` entries.
- Bearer tokens are redacted in content previews; previews truncated to 200 chars.
- **Known gap**: `Memory.search` FTS path does not filter `sm.redacted_at IS NULL`. See `docs/verification-boundaries.md`.

### Ledger Audit Trail

All mutation operations (save, correct, redact, hard delete, grant, revoke) emit ledger events when a ledger function is provided. Events record actor, scope, memory id, visibility, and sanitized content preview.

## GitHub App Integration

Clawq supports GitHub Apps for authentication, repository access, PR subscriptions, CI dispatch, review runs, and backlinks.

### Authentication

- **PAT mode**: Set `channels.github.auth` to a personal access token with `repo` scope.
- **GitHub App mode**: Use an installation token. The app must be installed on target repos with appropriate permissions (contents: read, pull requests: write, issues: write, checks: read).

### Repository Grants

Repo grants control which repos the agent can access. Each grant specifies:
- **Repo identifier**: `owner/repo` format or local path
- **Capabilities**: `Read`, `Write` (legacy `repositories` entries become read-only)
- **Codebase coverage**: Repo grants must intersect `codebase_grants` when codebase grants are non-empty

### PR Subscriptions

When a PR event arrives via webhook:
1. Webhook is verified (HMAC signature or app webhook secret)
2. Duplicate deliveries suppressed via LRU set keyed on `X-GitHub-Delivery`
3. `allow_users` gates events (except system events like `workflow_job`, `check_run`)
4. Session key: `github:{owner}/{repo}:pr:{n}` â€” repeated interactions on the same thread resume the same session
5. Comment lifecycle: eyes reaction â†’ placeholder comment â†’ async processing â†’ edit placeholder with final response
6. All bot replies include `<!-- clawq-reply -->` marker for self-loop protection

### CI Dispatch

GitHub Actions can be triggered by the agent via the checks API. The agent can inspect workflow runs, check suites, and check runs through webhook events.

### Review Runs

Background coding tasks can produce PR review comments. The `background finalize` workflow rebases, fast-forward merges, and cleans up the worktree. Completion notifications include git status (dirty/clean, commit count, rebased) and LLM-generated summaries.

### Backlinks

Hook payload snapshots are written to `~/.clawq/workspace/tmp/github-deliveries/` on every verified delivery. File-backed event hooks from `~/.clawq/workspace/gh-hooks/` allow custom processing with explicit `repo` and `event` frontmatter.

## Egress Policy

The egress evaluator controls policy-aware outbound HTTP access. Explicit scoped rules are evaluated first, then the top-level default allowlist, then the top-level strictness.

Top-level config:

- `egress.strictness`: `strict` denies unmatched policy-aware HTTP destinations; `permissive` allows unmatched destinations after explicit rules and the default allowlist are checked. `egress.default_policy` is accepted as an alias where `deny` maps to `strict` and `allow` maps to `permissive`.
- `egress.default_allowlist`: fallback egress rules evaluated after access-bundle rules. The default allows `GET https://clawq.org/llms.txt` and `GET https://clawq.org/llms-full.txt` so Clawq can read its own self-reference docs.

### Rules

Egress rules are defined in access bundles and merged with scope precedence (profile > room > channel > workspace > default). Each rule specifies:
- **Host pattern**: glob or exact match
- **Path pattern**: URL path glob
- **Method**: HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.)
- **Action**: `Allow` or `Deny`
- **Log policy**: `Log` or `No_log`

### Evaluator

- **Strict by default**: When no scoped rule or default-allowlist entry matches, `strict` denies and `permissive` allows.
- **First-match-wins**: Rules are evaluated in declaration order; the first matching rule determines the action.
- **Profile-first ordering**: Profile bundle rules come before scope rules. Within scope rules, order is room > channel > workspace > default.
- **HTTP-only boundary**: Egress rules apply to Clawq's policy-aware HTTP paths such as `web_fetch`, `web_search`, `http_get`, `http_request`, HTTP MCP startup/tool calls, and other callers of the policy HTTP client. They do not firewall arbitrary network access from shell commands.

### Audit

Egress decisions can be logged. Denied requests are always logged. Allowed requests follow the rule's `log_policy`.

## Credential Management

Clawq manages credentials through handles, provider configs, and a lease API.

### Credential Handles

Credentials are stored in config under provider-specific keys (`providers.*.api_key`, `channels.*.bot_token`, etc.). Secrets can be encrypted at rest using AES-256-GCM with `CLAWQ_MASTER_KEY`.

### Providers

Each provider has:
- `api_key` (required for API-key providers; OAuth providers use provider-specific credentials)
- `base_url` (provider-specific defaults built-in for openrouter, mistral, xai, deepseek, cohere, zai, kimi, minimax, xiaomi, etc.)
- `kind` (optional, for provider-specific request formatting)
- `default_model` (provider-specific model name, not `provider:model` format)

### Lease API

Runner bearer tokens are generated via `clawq runner token --session KEY [--ttl-hours N]`. Tokens are:
- Localhost-only
- SHA256-hashed in memory
- TTL-based (default 24h)
- Used for remote question relay via `/runner/ask` and MCP endpoints

The daemon auto-sets `CLAWQ_RUNNER_TOKEN`, `CLAWQ_MCP_URL`, and `CLAWQ_RUNNER_ASK_URL` env vars in background task runner processes.

### Redaction

Config display (`config show`, `config tree`) redacts all secrets. HTTP debug logs (`debug http`) redact tokens. Most connector credentials lack redaction in normal log paths (known infrastructure gap).

## Room Readiness Checks

The pilot setup wizard (`clawq rooms wizard`) runs readiness checks before applying changes. Checks include:

| Check | Validation |
|-------|------------|
| Profile ID | Lowercase alphanumeric with hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars |
| Model | Non-empty |
| Access Bundles | All referenced bundles exist in config |
| Connector Available | Connector has usable credentials configured |
| Connector Room | Room ID matches connector format (Teams: `19:` prefix or `@thread.tacv2` suffix; Slack: `C`/`G`/`D`/`#` prefix; Discord/Telegram: non-empty) |
| Budget | Limits are non-negative |
| Budget State | Current usage vs limits when a database profile exists |
| Budget Denial Msg | Redacted budget denial message does not leak configured limits (only when a budget is configured) |
| Max Tool Iterations | Between 1 and 1000 |
| Budget Reset Period | One of: daily, weekly, monthly, yearly |
| GitHub App | App ID, private key, and installations are valid |
| Repo Grants | Access bundles have valid `owner/repo` grants with capabilities |
| Webhook Config | Webhook secrets/paths are configured and a gateway or tunnel endpoint is present |
| Room Backlink | Profile has access bundles with repo grants |
| Activity Ledger | SQLite schema is accessible |
| Egress Audit | SQLite schema is accessible |

Readiness check results appear in `wizard rerun` output with status categories: **Changed** (will update), **Already valid** (no action), **Blocked** (missing dependency), **Manual repair** (needs human intervention).

## Invocation Restrictions

Room profiles can restrict agent invocation through tool allow/deny lists, token budgets, cost budgets, and iteration limits.

### Tool Policy

Per-profile `allowed_tools` and `denied_tools` lists are enforced at the tool registry level. Deny wins over allow. Card action buttons (Inspect, Continue, Cancel, Retry, View Logs, Finalize) check room policy before executing â€” buttons for denied tools are omitted from rendered cards.

### Budgets

Room budgets track cumulative usage (tokens, cost, turns) per profile per reset period:
- `token_limit` and `cost_limit_usd` (non-negative, 0 = disabled)
- `reset_period`: daily, weekly, monthly, yearly
- Soft warning at 80% (configurable)
- When limits exceeded, further requests are blocked (`limit_exceeded = true`)
- Budget period resets are explicit via `reset_profile_budget`

### Iteration Limits

`max_tool_iterations` (1-1000, default 25) caps the number of tool calls per agent turn.

## Teams Delivery Lifecycle and Progress Cards

Teams is the primary design target for room-origin background task delivery. The delivery lifecycle tracks every outbound message through granular states recorded to the room activity ledger.

### Lifecycle States

```
Scheduled -> Generated -> Attempted -> Transport_accepted -> Message_id_recorded
                                                                |
                                              Edit_failed ------+-> Fallback_sent
                                                                |
                                              Failed ------------+
                                                                |
                                              User_visible_unconfirmed
```

Each state transition includes a `tracking_id` (format: `dlv_<timestamp>_<random>`), task ID, room ID, connector name, and optional metadata (thread ID, activity ID, message ID, error message).

### Progress Checklists

The checklist model (`room_progress_checklist.ml`) is a durable, append-only model backed by SQLite. Each item tracks: `task_id`, `title`, `state`, `transcript_url`, `session_url`, `session_record_id`, `delivery_state`.

**Item states**: Planned (white square), Current (arrows), Blocked (prohibited), Done (check), Final (flag). State transitions reset delivery state to pending.

**Delivery states**: pending, sent, confirmed, failed.

### Teams Adaptive Cards

On Teams, progress is rendered as an Adaptive Card (v1.4) with:
- Color-coded header by overall state (blue = working, green = done, red = blocked)
- Summary line with compact count by state
- Elapsed time
- Checklist container with icons, titles, and clickable links
- Action buttons: Inspect, Continue, Cancel, Retry, View Logs, Finalize

Buttons are `Action.Submit` with `imBack` data values. Room policy is applied at render time â€” buttons for denied tools are omitted. Edit-in-place updates existing cards; fallback sends new card on edit failure.

### Slack Baseline

Slack renders checklists as formatted mrkdwn text (no Adaptive Cards). Links use Slack's `<url|label>` syntax. Supported features: edit, delete, reactions, status messages, native thread replies, ambient history capture.

### Rate Limiting

- **Inbound**: Per-conversation+user rate limiting with 60-second warning cooldowns.
- **Outbound**: Per-conversation throttling (1 req/s minimum interval) with exponential backoff retry (up to 3 attempts) on 429, 412, 502, 504.

### File Consent Flow

Teams file uploads use a consent card: filename + description + size â†’ user accepts/declines â†’ upload or temp download URL fallback.

## What-Can-Do Capability Introspection

The `/whatcando` command shows a real-time snapshot of room capabilities.

### Capabilities Reported

- **Connector capabilities**: edit, delete, reactions, typing, status, file sending, cards, buttons
- **Room state**: profile binding, history capture, history persistence, delivery mode, max message length
- **Readiness**: database availability, GitHub configuration
- **Degraded behaviors**: explanations for missing features

### Rendering

On Teams: Adaptive Card with FactSets. On other connectors: markdown text.

### Connector Capability Matrix

| Feature | Teams | Slack | Discord | IRC/Email/etc. |
|---------|-------|-------|---------|----------------|
| Edit messages | Yes | Yes | Yes | Varies |
| Delete messages | Yes | Yes | Yes | Varies |
| Reactions | No | Yes | Yes | Varies |
| Typing indicator | Yes | No | No | No |
| Adaptive Cards | Yes | No | No | No |
| Buttons | Yes | No | No | No |
| Thread replies | Thread-like | Native | Native | Varies |
| Max message length | 28672 | 4000 | 2000 | Varies |

Progress updates are only dispatched for Slack, Teams, and Discord. All other connectors receive no progress updates (dispatch returns `None`).

## Heartbeats

- Heartbeats are controlled by `heartbeat.enabled`, `heartbeat.interval_seconds`, `heartbeat.quiet_start`, and `heartbeat.quiet_end`.
- `heartbeat.enabled` is the global master switch. Session participation is a separate persisted opt-in.
- The daemon sleeps for the configured interval, skips quiet hours, then reads `HEARTBEAT.md` from the workspace root.
- If `HEARTBEAT.md` is missing or empty, nothing happens.
- Heartbeat execution targets only sessions explicitly opted in via `clawq session heartbeat SESSION on` or `/heartbeat on` from a supported chat session.
- Supported heartbeat sessions are Telegram, Slack, Discord, and Teams session keys; web and `__main__` are not valid heartbeat targets.
- Each opted-in session is processed independently. If one target session is busy, that session's tick is skipped rather than queued; other opted-in sessions still run.
- The heartbeat prompt content is literally the trimmed contents of `HEARTBEAT.md`.
- In the runtime-context block, "heartbeat routing enabled for this session" means global heartbeat is enabled and this specific session is opted in for heartbeat delivery.
- If the model replies exactly `HEARTBEAT_OK`, the daemon logs success and sends no outbound message.
- Otherwise the daemon delivers the reply through the session's registered notifier or channel dispatch fallback for that opted-in session.
- Heartbeat turns run inside the opted-in session's own history and lock, not in `__main__`.

## Restart and Resume Model

- There are two daemon launch modes in practice: service-managed (`clawq service start`) and direct agent execution (`clawq agent`). They share most runtime code but not identical operational control paths.
- `clawq service signal-restart` only targets the daemon discovered via the service PID file. If no PID file exists, that command reports "Daemon is not running" even if a directly launched `clawq agent` process is alive and reflected in `daemon_state.json`.
- `SIGUSR1` on the live daemon process triggers graceful restart logic: the daemon enters draining mode, interrupts all active sessions (so long-running tool calls like `background_task_wait` can detect imminent restart and return early), warns active channel sessions, waits for in-flight requests to finish, then restarts itself.
- Drain warnings go to currently registered live channel notifiers immediately, then again at 5s, 10s, 15s, 30s, 45s, and 60s while waiting.
- If drain does not finish within about 60 seconds, the daemon forces the restart.
- The actual restart path is an in-place `execve` re-entry. In service-managed mode this goes back through `clawq service start` with internal no-fork environment flags; direct `clawq agent` restart uses the agent re-exec path instead.
- `clawq service restart` is different: it is stop, sleep 1 second, start. That path is not graceful and is meaningful only for service-managed daemon instances.
- Persisted service/runtime state lives under `~/.clawq/`, especially `daemon.pid`, `daemon.pid.meta`, `daemon.log`, and `daemon_state.json`.
- `daemon_state.json` is runtime state, not authoritative proof that service commands can control the process. The service PID file governs `clawq service stop|restart|signal-restart`; `daemon_state.json` is primarily for status/introspection.

### What resumes after restart

- Conversation history is stored in SQLite (`memory.db` by default unless `memory.db_path` overrides it).
- Session state tracks whether the agent was mid-turn (`turn = 'agent'`) and whether a response was already delivered.
- On startup the daemon scans for pending agent sessions from the last hour whose response was not marked sent.
- Automatic post-restart completion currently resumes only sessions that have persisted channel metadata for a supported outbound channel: Telegram, Discord, or Slack.
- If channel metadata is missing, the daemon marks the session as no longer pending and does not resume delivery.
- After pending agent session resume, the durable inbound queue is replayed: stale claims (>3600 s) and failed rows are reclaimed, then pending rows are processed FIFO per session through Session.turn with at-least-once delivery.
- A separate `restart_notify.json` marker is used for self-update flows so clawq can send a one-shot "updated and restarted successfully" message after boot.
- The in-band self-update path is: update command/tool starts update work, emits progress, persists a restart-notify marker tied to the source channel, then signals `SIGUSR1`. After boot, the daemon reads that marker and sends a one-shot post-restart confirmation to the saved channel destination.
- This is a handoff model, not a continuous same-turn monologue. The pre-restart turn ends after the restart request is prepared; confirmation arrives from the replacement process after startup.
- Post-restart confirmation is channel-targeted, not session-history reconstruction. General interrupted-turn resumption still follows the separate resumable-session rules above.
- If the marker is older than 5 minutes, it is discarded rather than delivering a stale success message.

### Autostart on reboot

- `clawq service install` writes a platform-specific service definition and enables autostart. On Linux this creates a systemd user unit at `~/.config/systemd/user/clawq.service` and runs `systemctl --user enable clawq`. On macOS it writes a launchd plist to `~/Library/LaunchAgents/org.clawq.daemon.plist` with `RunAtLoad` enabled.
- The systemd unit uses `Type=forking` and defers process management to clawq's own double-fork. The launchd plist sets `CLAWQ_DAEMON_NOFORK=1` so launchd manages the process directly.
- For the systemd user unit to survive logout, the user must also run `loginctl enable-linger $USER`.
- To disable autostart without removing the service definition: on Linux run `systemctl --user disable clawq`; on macOS run `launchctl unload ~/Library/LaunchAgents/org.clawq.daemon.plist`.
- `clawq service uninstall` removes the service definition entirely and stops the daemon.
- `clawq service status` shows the current autostart state.
- Installing the service does not interfere with an already-running daemon. The systemd unit or launchd plist will manage future starts; the current process continues until stopped.

### Runtime reload and tool registration caveats

- The daemon supports config reload both on `SIGHUP` and via file-mtime polling (every 10 seconds).
- Reload updates the in-memory runtime config used by active and future sessions.
- This means settings such as provider defaults, memory limits, heartbeat timing, and `web_search` configuration are intended to apply without a restart.
- Startup-structural settings still require restart: enabling or disabling channels, changing gateway bind host/port, and similar boot-time wiring.
- Tool registration itself is not fully rebuilt on each reload. Conditional tools that depend on startup wiring remain governed by how the daemon was initialized.
- In particular, `send_message` and `ask_user_question` depend on runtime wiring, not config alone.

## Autonomous Continuation and Proactive Messaging

- Clawq can resume some interrupted agent turns after restart, but there is no general "re-prompt every unfinished idea" system.
- Resume depends on persisted session state plus a supported channel destination.
- Heartbeats are the built-in periodic autonomous mechanism.
- The `send_message` built-in tool exists only when the runtime registers it with a send function. Configuring `notify.notify_channel` / `notify.notify_target` alone does not guarantee the tool is available.
- When invoked during an active channel turn, `send_message` prefers the live session notifier from tool context, so the message goes straight back to the current attached session.
- Outside an active notified session, behavior falls back to the runtime-provided send function if one exists; if no send function was wired at startup, the tool is absent or returns an error.
- Heartbeat replies and continuation follow-ups are delivered through the opted-in session's normal outbound channel path.
- During an active channel turn, the runtime can send additional text to that same session via the registered notifier.
- Turn ownership is effectively per session key: one session runs at a time under a mutex, while different session keys can proceed independently.

## Runtime Architecture Summary

- Daemon/service layer: `clawq agent` or `clawq service start` runs the long-lived OCaml daemon.
- Channels: HTTP/Web UI plus channel adapters (Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.) convert inbound events into session turns.
- Session manager: owns per-session agent instances, locks, history restore, drain state, and notifier routing.
- Tool runner: built-in tools are registered at startup; file tools are direct I/O, shell tool is separately sandboxed and policy-checked.
- Sandbox layer: Firejail/Bubblewrap/none wraps shell execution only.
- Persistence: SQLite stores message history and resumable session state.
- Scheduler/heartbeat: cron jobs run from the database with optional TTL (`--ttl 24h`, `--ttl 7d`, etc.) that auto-disables jobs after expiration; when a cron job fires it first posts the prompt into chat (prefixed with `[cron:JOB_NAME]`) then runs the LLM turn and delivers the response. Schedule format: "every 5m" (interval) or standard 5-field cron. Ephemeral cron jobs (`--ephemeral`) run in an isolated session via the Local runner in the background task system, with results delivered back to the main session. Heartbeat polling reads `HEARTBEAT.md` and fans out across opted-in Telegram/Slack/Discord/Teams sessions.
- Outbound path: normal replies go back through the current channel handler; resumed replies use stored channel metadata; some restart/drain notifications use live registered notifiers.

## Your Channels

| Channel | How it connects | Status |
|---------|----------------|--------|
| Web UI | HTTP gateway at host:port, SSE streaming | Implemented |
| Telegram | Bot API long polling â€” no public IP needed | Implemented |
| Discord | Gateway v10 WebSocket + REST API â€” auto heartbeat/resume/reconnect; guild messages filtered to @mention/name/command only | Implemented |
| Slack (Socket Mode) | WSS via app_token â€” no public IP needed | Implemented |
| Slack (Events API) | HTTP push to configurable path (default /slack/events) | Implemented |
| WhatsApp | Cloud API webhook at /whatsapp/webhook | Implemented |
| LINE | Messaging API webhook at /line/webhook | Implemented |
| Lark/Feishu | Bot webhook at /lark/webhook | Implemented |
| GitHub | Webhook at configurable paths per repo, plus hook files under `~/.clawq/workspace/gh-hooks/` | Implemented |
| Mattermost | API polling / HTTP API integration | Implemented |
| Signal | signal-cli-rest-api compatible polling/HTTP integration | Implemented |
| Matrix | Matrix client sync/send integration | Implemented |
| IRC | IRC client connection and channel listening | Implemented |
| Email | IMAP polling + SMTP replies | Implemented |
| Nostr | Relay websocket integration | Implemented |
| DingTalk | DingTalk bot integration | Implemented |
| iMessage | Local Messages database polling on macOS | Implemented |
| OneBot | OneBot websocket/HTTP integration | Implemented |
| MS Teams | Bot Framework webhook at configurable path (default /teams/webhook) | Implemented |

### Voice Transcription (All Channels)

When an STT provider is configured, audio attachments sent to any channel (Telegram, Discord, Slack, Teams) are automatically transcribed. The bot sends a "Transcribing..." progress message, updates it to "Transcribing... Done!" on completion, and prepends the transcribed text as `[Voice]: <text>` to the message.

Validation limits:
- File size: under 25 MB
- Duration: under 1 hour (when metadata available, e.g. Telegram)
- Music detection: `.ogg`, `.opus`, `.webm` are treated as voice; `.mp3`, `.m4a`, `.aac`, `.wma`, `.flac` are treated as music unless the filename contains "voice", "recording", or "audio_message" (case-insensitive)
- Requires `stt` config section with `provider` and `model`

### Setting up Telegram
1. User creates a bot with @BotFather on Telegram
2. `clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.bot_token "TOKEN"`
3. `clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.allow_from '["*"]'`
4. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up Discord
1. User creates app at Discord Developer Portal, adds Bot, copies token
2. User invites bot with `applications.commands` + `bot` scopes, `Send Messages` + `Read Message History` permissions
3. `clawq config set channels.discord.bot_token "TOKEN"`
4. `clawq config set channels.discord.allow_guilds '["*"]'`
5. `clawq config set channels.discord.allow_users '["*"]'`
6. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up Slack (Socket Mode, recommended)
1. User creates app at api.slack.com/apps, enables Socket Mode
2. User generates App-Level Token (xapp-...) with connections:write scope
3. User installs app, copies Bot Token (xoxb-...)
4. User subscribes to message.channels and message.im events
5. `clawq config set channels.slack.bot_token "xoxb-..."`
6. `clawq config set channels.slack.app_token "xapp-..."`
7. `clawq config set channels.slack.socket_mode true`
8. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up Slack (Events API)
1. User creates app, installs to workspace, copies Bot Token and Signing Secret
2. `clawq config set channels.slack.bot_token "xoxb-..."`
3. `clawq config set channels.slack.signing_secret "abc123..."`
4. `clawq config set channels.slack.socket_mode false`
5. Ensure gateway is publicly reachable (via tunnel or direct)
6. Set Slack Event Subscription URL to `https://your-domain/slack/events`
7. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up MS Teams
0. Start clawq with a tunnel first to get a public HTTPS URL: `clawq config set tunnel.enabled true && clawq agent`
1. Register an app in Azure Active Directory; note Application (client) ID â†’ app_id, Directory (tenant) ID â†’ tenant_id
2. Create a client secret â†’ app_secret (expires in 12-24 months; rotate before expiry)
3. Register a Bot in Azure Bot Service; set Messaging Endpoint to `https://your-tunnel-url/teams/webhook` (must be HTTPS)
4. Set Microsoft App ID on the bot to the AAD app_id
5. Add Microsoft Teams channel in Azure Bot Service Channels blade
6. **Go to Teams Developer Portal (https://dev.teams.microsoft.com/apps/) â†’ create app manifest â†’ add bot using app_id â†’ publish/install to tenant** â€” required; without this the bot is invisible in Teams. If publishing to your org, an admin must approve the app in the Teams Admin Center (https://admin.teams.microsoft.com/policies/manage-apps/). When installing to an org, search for the bot by name in the Teams app store â€” this is the easiest way to find it.
7. `clawq config set channels.teams.app_id "your-app-id"`
8. `clawq config set channels.teams.app_secret "your-secret"`
9. `clawq config set channels.teams.tenant_id "your-tenant-id"`
10. `clawq channel test teams` â€” verifies OAuth credentials live
11. `clawq agent` to start

**channels.teams** config fields: `app_id`, `app_secret`, `tenant_id`, `webhook_path` (default `/teams/webhook`), `service_url` (default `https://smba.trafficmanager.net/amer`), `allow_teams` (default `["*"]`), `allow_users` (default `["*"]`).

Session key format: `teams:<team_id_or_personal>:<conversation_id>` (shared per-channel; sender identity via context headers)

In group chats (`conversation.isGroup`), the bot only responds when explicitly @mentioned, addressed by name (e.g. "clawq, ..."), or sent a slash command. When it does reply, the sender is @mentioned using Bot Framework mention entities so they receive a notification.

Note: JWT RS256 signature verification is not implemented (no RSA lib available). Claim-only validation (`aud`, `iss`, `exp`, `nbf`) is performed.

## Gateway and Web UI

The daemon serves the web UI and JSON/SSE gateway at `http://host:port/`. Main routes:

| Endpoint | Method | Description |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| `/`, `/ui`, `/index.html`, `/ui/index.html` | GET | Web UI HTML shell |
| `/chat.js`, `/ui/chat.js`, `/chat.css`, `/ui/chat.css` | GET | Embedded UI assets |
| `/chat` | POST | Synchronous chat (non-streaming) |
| `/chat/stream` | POST | SSE chat streaming |
| `/session/inject` | POST | Inject a live inbound message into an arbitrary session key via the daemon session manager |
| `/health` | GET | Health check â€” returns `{"status":"ok"}` |
| `/commands` | GET | Slash-command metadata for autocomplete |
| `/ui-version` | GET | UI bundle version |
| `/pair` | GET | Pairing status: current code, attempts, lock state, paired count |
| `/pair` | POST | Exchange 6-digit OTP for bearer token |
| `/pair/regenerate` | POST | Regenerate pairing code (requires valid auth or paired token) |
| `/slack/events` | POST | Slack Events API webhook (path configurable) |
| `/whatsapp/webhook` | GET/POST | WhatsApp webhook (verification + messages) |
| `/line/webhook` | POST | LINE webhook |
| `/lark/webhook` | POST | Lark webhook |
| `/teams/webhook` (configurable) | POST | MS Teams Bot Framework webhook |
| GitHub repo paths | POST | GitHub webhooks (paths configured per repo); hook payload snapshots written under `~/.clawq/workspace/tmp/github-deliveries/` |
| `web_channel.path_prefix...` | GET/POST/etc. | Optional WebChannel routes when `web_channel.enabled = true` |

Gateway auth behavior:

- `GET /`, `/ui*`, `/chat.js`, `/chat.css`, `/commands`, `/health`, and `/ui-version` are unauthenticated.
- `POST /chat` and `POST /chat/stream` require a paired token when `gateway.require_pairing = true`; otherwise they accept anonymous requests unless `gateway.auth_token` is set.
- `POST /session/inject` follows the same auth rules as the JSON chat endpoints and is intended for local CLI-driven session control.
- When `gateway.auth_token` is set, JSON chat endpoints also accept `Authorization: Bearer <token>` or `x-api-key: <token>`.
- `GET /pair` exposes pairing status only when pairing is configured.
- `POST /pair` exchanges a valid code for a bearer token.
- `POST /pair/regenerate` requires existing auth and is unavailable if pairing is disabled.
- Slack, LINE, Lark, GitHub, WhatsApp, and Teams webhook routes have their own signature/verification handling in addition to normal gateway auth decisions.
- GitHub webhook delivery also enforces that the payload repo identity matches the configured `channels.github.repos[].name` for that webhook path.
- GitHub rejects ambiguous webhook path bindings with `409`, so `channels.github.repos[].webhook_path` values must be unique.
- GitHub also supports file-backed event hooks from `~/.clawq/workspace/gh-hooks/`; hooks require explicit `repo` and `event` frontmatter and currently use exact-match `match:` fields.
- GitHub hook payload snapshots are written on every verified webhook delivery, cleaned up opportunistically on new deliveries, and exposed to the agent via `payload_path` plus inline fallback JSON in the rendered prompt.
- `channels.github.repos[].allow_users` gates all GitHub webhook events except a small explicit system-event subset (`workflow_job`, `workflow_run`, `check_run`, `check_suite`, `ping`), so events with arbitrary user-controlled text such as `push` remain allowlisted.
- GET {teams.webhook_path} returns a JSON status document when Teams is configured: {"status":"ready","channel":"teams","webhook_path":"...","app_id_prefix":"..."}
- Unknown routes return `404 {"error":"not found"}`.

## First-Time Setup Guide

Tell users:
1. Install Node.js/npm if needed.
2. `npm install -g @clawq/clawq` â€” installs the packaged `clawq` binary.
3. `clawq version` â€” verify the CLI is on PATH. Use `npx @clawq/clawq version` when testing without a global install.
4. `clawq onboard` â€” creates initial config; in a TTY it runs onboarding, otherwise it writes a starter template. Use `clawq config wizard` later for full reconfiguration. For individual channels, use `clawq setup <channel>` (discord, telegram, slack, github, teams, irc, matrix, mattermost, email, dingtalk, lark, line, nostr, onebot, whatsapp, imessage, signal, tunnel, summarizer).
5. `clawq doctor` â€” validate configuration
6. `clawq agent` â€” start the daemon
7. Open `http://127.0.0.1:13451/` for the web UI
8. Use `clawq otp-show` to get the pairing code if pairing is enabled

The current npm release artifact is built on Linux. On other platforms, use the source build path for now: clone the repo, install opam plus system SQLite headers, run `make bootstrap`, then `make build` and `make test`.

## Pilot Setup Wizard

The `clawq rooms wizard` command configures room-agent profiles with a plan/apply/rerun flow. Teams-first rollout with Slack baseline support.

### Modes

| Mode | Admin Required | Description |
|------|---------------|-------------|
| `interactive` (default) | Yes (`CLAWQ_ADMIN=1`) | Prompts for all configuration options |
| `plan` | No | Read-only; shows what would happen |
| `apply` | Yes | Applies configuration changes |
| `rerun` | No (read); Yes (`--apply`) | Compares desired state vs current config |

### Rerun Status Categories

- **Changed** â€” value differs from current config; will be updated
- **Already valid** â€” value matches; no action needed
- **Blocked** â€” missing dependency (e.g., access bundles not created, connector not configured)
- **Manual repair** â€” needs human intervention (e.g., invalid room format, inconsistent budget)

### Options

`--profile-id`, `--model`, `--system-prompt`, `--max-iters`, `--allowed-tools`, `--denied-tools`, `--access-bundles`, `--token-limit`, `--cost-limit`, `--reset-period`, `--connector` (default: teams), `--room`, `--inactive`, `--apply` (rerun only).

### Merge Semantics

Default values preserve existing config. Explicit values override. Empty values preserve existing. Safe to re-run with only the fields you want to change.

### Usage

```bash
# Interactive
CLAWQ_ADMIN=1 clawq rooms wizard

# Plan (read-only, use '--' before wizard options)
clawq rooms -- wizard plan --profile-id my-agent --connector teams --room "19:abc@thread.tacv2"

# Apply
CLAWQ_ADMIN=1 clawq rooms -- wizard apply --profile-id my-agent --connector teams --room "19:abc@thread.tacv2"

# Rerun with auto-apply
CLAWQ_ADMIN=1 clawq rooms -- wizard rerun --profile-id my-agent --connector teams --room "19:abc@thread.tacv2" --apply
```

Not available in the minimal build (`clawq-min`). Requires the full `clawq` binary.

## Troubleshooting

### Self-diagnostics checklist

1. Identify the path and session involved: workspace path (`clawq workspace`), session key shape (`web:...`, `telegram:...`, `__main__`, etc.), and channel.
2. Inspect security posture: `clawq config show security` and note `workspace_only`, `sandbox_backend`, and `extra_allowed_paths`.
3. Inspect daemon state: `clawq service status`, `clawq status`, `~/.clawq/daemon.log`, `~/.clawq/daemon_state.json`. Distinguish service PID-file state from general runtime state.
4. Distinguish path-policy errors from shell-semantics errors: file tools use direct OCaml I/O; shell may still reject commands because of allowlist/direct-exec rules even when the filesystem view matches.
5. For restart/resume issues, inspect whether the session had channel metadata and whether the response was marked sent in SQLite state.

### Common runtime-specific failures

- **Shell cannot access `~/src`, but file tools can**: if `workspace_only = true`, first check whether `~/src` is in `security.extra_allowed_paths`; if `workspace_only = false`, suspect shell command semantics rather than filesystem visibility.
- **`cd ~/src/clawq && ...` fails**: in default `workspace_only` mode, `cd` and `&&` are not supported because execution is direct-process style and unsafe shell syntax is rejected. Use a direct allowed binary with explicit path arguments, or disable `workspace_only` if you truly need shell semantics.
- **Shell builtin fails with `No such file or directory`**: you are likely in direct-exec mode, not `/bin/sh -c` mode.
- **Git or tools complain about missing `/dev/null`**: inspect sandbox backend. Bubblewrap tries to bind `/dev` and `/dev/null`; if this still fails, verify host `/dev/null` exists and try `security.sandbox_backend = "none"` or `"firejail"` to isolate the backend problem.
- **Heartbeats expected but not appearing**: confirm `heartbeat.enabled = true`, quiet hours are not suppressing the tick, `HEARTBEAT.md` exists and is non-empty, and the target session was opted in with `clawq session heartbeat SESSION on` or `/heartbeat on`. Also verify the session is a supported Telegram/Slack/Discord/Teams session.
- **A turn seems stuck or you want to interrupt it from chat**: send a message starting with `!`. Example: `!stop` interrupts the in-flight turn for that session and then sends `stop` as the next normal message; a bare `!` becomes `[interrupted]`.
- **`clawq service signal-restart` says daemon is not running, but `daemon_state.json` shows a live pid**: you are likely running a direct `clawq agent` process rather than a service-managed daemon. `clawq service ...` commands follow the service PID file, not `daemon_state.json`.
- **Need graceful restart for a direct `clawq agent` process**: send `SIGUSR1` to the live process itself; do not assume `clawq service signal-restart` can reach it unless it was started through the service path.
- **Service restarted but autonomous work did not resume**: only recent pending sessions with stored channel + channel_id for Telegram/Discord/Slack are resumed automatically.
- **Behavior differs because session is not opted into heartbeat**: normal user chats use their own session keys and histories; heartbeat runs only for sessions explicitly opted in.
- **Bot not responding**: check daemon is running (`clawq status`), verify token (`clawq doctor`), check allow_from/allow_users/allow_guilds includes the user or is `["*"]`.
- **Daemon refuses to bind non-loopback host**: if `gateway.host` is not loopback, set `gateway.auth_token` or change `gateway.host` back to `127.0.0.1`/`localhost`.
- **No providers configured**: ensure `providers` section exists in config with either a valid api_key or a configured subscription provider such as `openai-codex`, and `agent_defaults.primary_model` is set to a `provider:model` string (e.g. `"openrouter:openai/gpt-4o"` or `"openai-codex:gpt-5.4"`).
- **LLM API errors**: verify API key validity, try a different model, check base_url matches provider.
- **Config not found**: run `clawq onboard` to create the initial config, or `clawq config wizard` after a config already exists.
- **`clawq rooms wizard` requires admin**: set `CLAWQ_ADMIN=1` in your environment. Plan mode is read-only and does not require admin.
- **Wizard shows "Connector not configured"**: the connector you specified doesn't have usable credentials. Run `clawq config show channels` to verify. Teams requires `app_id` and `app_secret`; Slack requires `bot_token` and `signing_secret`.
- **Wizard shows "Room ID format is invalid"**: each connector has specific room ID requirements. Teams: must start with `19:` or end with `@thread.tacv2`. Slack: must start with `C`, `G`, `D`, or `#`.
- **Wizard rerun shows "Blocked" items**: missing dependencies (e.g., access bundles not created, connector not configured). Resolve the dependency first, then re-run.
- **Wizard not available in minimal build**: the pilot setup wizard requires the full `clawq` binary. Use `clawq` instead of `clawq-min`.
- **Check health**: `curl http://127.0.0.1:13451/health`.
- **Pairing confusion in browser**: use `clawq otp-show`, then `GET /pair` to inspect pairing status if needed; `POST /pair/regenerate` requires existing auth.
- **Reset state**: `clawq reset-agent` (wipes sessions + cron + workspace) or `clawq reset-workspace` (lighter, keeps cron). Both auto-backup workspace files before resetting; restore with `clawq workspace restore <name>`.
- **Backup/restore workspace**: `clawq workspace backup my-snapshot` to save, `clawq workspace versions` to list, `clawq workspace restore my-snapshot` to restore.

## Docs

- [Quick Start](https://clawq.org/quickstart): Install from npm, configure, and run clawq in 5 minutes
- [Architecture](https://clawq.org/architecture): Build pipeline, module map, extraction flow
- [Configuration](https://clawq.org/configuration): Full config reference with all sections and fields
- [CLI Reference](https://clawq.org/cli-reference): Complete command reference
- [Channels](https://clawq.org/channels): Channel setup and daemon startup registry
- [Development Guide](https://clawq.org/development): Build, test, format, contribute
- [Verification Status](https://clawq.org/formal-verification): Machine-checked Coq verification status (22 verified phases, 374 theorems across 27 Coq files; 1 Admitted remaining)
- [Chat Log Viewer](https://clawq.org/chat-viewer): Client-side browser tool to inspect .json chat logs from `clawq session show` or Claude Code â€” renders messages, tool calls, tool responses, and thinking blocks

Preferred Codex delegation model: `gpt-5.4` when quality matters. Use `gpt-5.3-codex` when you specifically want the Codex-tuned path or need to mirror Codex OAuth defaults more closely.
